**Security Rule:** Untrusted input must be decoded or unescaped into a canonical form exactly once, only when encoded data in that form is expected, and before it is validated, sanitized, or otherwise processed.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Input is decoded more than once, or decoded again after it has been validated or sanitized, so a payload that appeared safe when checked is transformed back into a dangerous value before it is used.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Input is decoded a single time at the trust boundary, before validation and sanitization, and is never decoded again afterwards.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var v = Uri.UnescapeDataString(Uri.UnescapeDataString(input));
```
Secure:
```
var v = Uri.UnescapeDataString(input);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
clean = sanitize(decode(input))
use(decode(clean))            # second decode revives the payload
```
Secure:
```
decoded = decode(input)       # decode once, up front
use(sanitize(decoded))
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String v = URLDecoder.decode(URLDecoder.decode(in, UTF_8), UTF_8);
```
Secure:
```
String v = URLDecoder.decode(in, StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const value = decodeURIComponent(decodeURIComponent(input));
```
Secure:
```
const value = decodeURIComponent(input);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
data = unquote(unquote(request.args["q"]))
```
Secure:
```
data = unquote(request.args["q"])   # single decode before validation
```
embed_text:
Untrusted input must be decoded or unescaped into a canonical form exactly once, only when encoded data in that form is expected, and before it is validated, sanitized, or otherwise processed.
Input is decoded more than once, or decoded again after it has been validated or sanitized, so a payload that appeared safe when checked is transformed back into a dangerous value before it is used.
Input is decoded a single time at the trust boundary, before validation and sanitization, and is never decoded again afterwards.

**Security Rule:** Output encoding and escaping must be applied as the final step before data reaches the interpreter it is intended for, or be performed by that interpreter itself. **Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Data is encoded early and then further concatenated or transformed before it reaches the interpreter, so the encoding no longer matches the final output context and protection is lost. **Secure Pattern (Description):** Encoding is applied at the point of output, immediately before the value is handed to the interpreter, or the interpreter's own parameterized API performs the escaping. [[CSHARP PATTERNS]] Vulnerable: ``` var s = HttpUtility.HtmlEncode(value); Response.Write(""); ``` Secure: ``` Response.Write(""); ``` [[GENERIC PATTERNS]] Vulnerable: ``` safe = htmlEncode(name) page = "
" # re-used in a different context ``` Secure: ``` page = "
" ``` [[JAVA PATTERNS]] Vulnerable: ``` String cell = Encode.forHtml(value); out.print(""); ``` Secure: ``` out.print(""); ``` [[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]] Vulnerable: ``` const row = "" + escapeHtml(value) + "" + extra; el.innerHTML = row; ``` Secure: ``` el.textContent = value; # the DOM API encodes for this context ``` [[PYTHON PATTERNS]] Vulnerable: ``` encoded = html.escape(comment) return Markup("

" + encoded + "

" + raw_footer) ``` Secure: ``` return render_template("comment.html", comment=comment) # autoescape ```
embed_text:
Output encoding and escaping must be applied as the final step before data reaches the interpreter it is intended for, or be performed by that interpreter itself.
Data is encoded early and then further concatenated or transformed before it reaches the interpreter, so the encoding no longer matches the final output context and protection is lost.
Encoding is applied at the point of output, immediately before the value is handed to the interpreter, or the interpreter's own parameterized API performs the escaping.

**Security Rule:** Output encoding for an HTTP response, HTML, or XML document must match the exact context the data lands in (HTML element, attribute, comment, CSS, or HTTP header) so it cannot alter document structure. **Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A single generic encoder, or none, is used regardless of where the value is placed, so input that is inert in one context becomes active markup or a structural break in another. **Secure Pattern (Description):** A context-aware encoder is selected for each sink — HTML body, attribute, JavaScript, CSS, or URL — and applied where the value is written, ideally by an auto-escaping template engine. [[CSHARP PATTERNS]] Vulnerable: ``` Response.Write("

" + message + "

"); ``` Secure: ``` Response.Write("

" + HttpUtility.HtmlEncode(message) + "

"); ``` [[GENERIC PATTERNS]] Vulnerable: ``` html = "" ``` Secure: ``` html = "" ``` [[JAVA PATTERNS]] Vulnerable: ``` response.getWriter().write("

" + message + "

"); ``` Secure: ``` response.getWriter().write("

" + Encode.forHtml(message) + "

"); ``` [[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]] Vulnerable: ``` div.innerHTML = "Welcome " + username; ``` Secure: ``` div.textContent = "Welcome " + username; ``` [[PYTHON PATTERNS]] Vulnerable: ``` return HttpResponse("

" + title + "

") ``` Secure: ``` return render(request, "page.html", {"title": title}) # autoescape on ```
embed_text:
Output encoding for an HTTP response, HTML, or XML document must match the exact context the data lands in (HTML element, attribute, comment, CSS, or HTTP header) so it cannot alter document structure.
A single generic encoder, or none, is used regardless of where the value is placed, so input that is inert in one context becomes active markup or a structural break in another.
A context-aware encoder is selected for each sink — HTML body, attribute, JavaScript, CSS, or URL — and applied where the value is written, ideally by an auto-escaping template engine.

**Security Rule:** When URLs are built dynamically, untrusted data must be encoded for its URL context, and only safe protocols may be permitted so that javascript: or data: URLs cannot be introduced.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted data is concatenated into a URL without URL encoding, or a user-controlled value supplies the whole URL or its scheme, allowing a javascript: or data: URL to execute when followed.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Query and path values are URL encoded, and the final URL scheme is checked against an allowlist of safe protocols such as https and mailto before the URL is emitted or followed.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var url = "/view?id=" + id;
```
Secure:
```
var url = "/view?id=" + Uri.EscapeDataString(id);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
link = "/search?q=" + userInput
anchor = "<a href=" + userSuppliedUrl + ">"
```
Secure:
```
link = "/search?q=" + urlEncode(userInput)
if scheme(userSuppliedUrl) in {"http", "https"}: emit(userSuppliedUrl)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String url = "/view?id=" + id;
```
Secure:
```
String url = "/view?id=" + URLEncoder.encode(id, StandardCharsets.UTF_8);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
anchor.href = userInput;
```
Secure:
```
const u = new URL(userInput, location.origin);
if (u.protocol === "https:" || u.protocol === "http:") anchor.href = u.href;
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
url = "/go?next=" + request.args["next"]
```
Secure:
```
from urllib.parse import quote
url = "/go?next=" + quote(request.args["next"], safe="")
```
embed_text:
When URLs are built dynamically, untrusted data must be encoded for its URL context, and only safe protocols may be permitted so that javascript: or data: URLs cannot be introduced.
Untrusted data is concatenated into a URL without URL encoding, or a user-controlled value supplies the whole URL or its scheme, allowing a javascript: or data: URL to execute when followed.
Query and path values are URL encoded, and the final URL scheme is checked against an allowlist of safe protocols such as https and mailto before the URL is emitted or followed.

**Security Rule:** Output encoding or escaping must be used when JavaScript content, including JSON, is built dynamically, so untrusted data cannot change the structure of the script or document.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted data is concatenated directly into inline script or a JSON string, letting an attacker close the string or block and inject executable JavaScript.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Values are passed as data rather than concatenated into code — JSON is produced by a real serializer, and script needing server data reads it from a safely encoded data attribute or element.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var js = "<script>init('" + id + "')</script>";
```
Secure:
```
var js = "<script>init(" + JsonSerializer.Serialize(id) + ")</script>";
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
script = "var user = '" + name + "';"
```
Secure:
```
script = "var user = " + jsonSerialize(name) + ";"
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String js = "<script>load('" + id + "')</script>";
```
Secure:
```
String js = "<script>load(" + Encode.forJavaScript(id) + ")</script>";
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const payload = '{"name":"' + name + '"}';
```
Secure:
```
const payload = JSON.stringify({ name });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
body = "<script>var u='" + name + "'</script>"
```
Secure:
```
body = '<script>var u=' + json.dumps(name) + '</script>'
```
embed_text:
Output encoding or escaping must be used when JavaScript content, including JSON, is built dynamically, so untrusted data cannot change the structure of the script or document.
Untrusted data is concatenated directly into inline script or a JSON string, letting an attacker close the string or block and inject executable JavaScript.
Values are passed as data rather than concatenated into code — JSON is produced by a real serializer, and script needing server data reads it from a safely encoded data attribute or element.

**Security Rule:** Data selection and database queries (SQL, HQL, NoSQL, Cypher), including stored procedures, must use parameterized queries, an ORM, or an entity framework so untrusted input cannot alter the query.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted input is concatenated or interpolated into the query string, so the input is parsed as query syntax and an attacker can add clauses, change logic, or read or modify other data.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The query text is fixed and untrusted values are supplied only as bound parameters, or an ORM or query builder binds them, so input is always treated as data and never as query structure.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
cmd.CommandText = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + id;
```
Secure:
```
cmd.CommandText = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = @id";
cmd.Parameters.AddWithValue("@id", id);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '" + userInput + "'"
```
Secure:
```
query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?"
execute(query, [userInput])
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
stmt.executeQuery("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + id);
```
Secure:
```
var ps = conn.prepareStatement("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?");
ps.setString(1, id);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = '" + email + "'");
```
Secure:
```
db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ?", [email]);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + user_id)
```
Secure:
```
cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s", (user_id,))
```
embed_text:
Data selection and database queries (SQL, HQL, NoSQL, Cypher), including stored procedures, must use parameterized queries, an ORM, or an entity framework so untrusted input cannot alter the query.
Untrusted input is concatenated or interpolated into the query string, so the input is parsed as query syntax and an attacker can add clauses, change logic, or read or modify other data.
The query text is fixed and untrusted values are supplied only as bound parameters, or an ORM or query builder binds them, so input is always treated as data and never as query structure.

**Security Rule:** The application must protect against OS command injection by using parameterized OS calls — passing arguments as a vector — or contextual command-line encoding, and must avoid invoking a shell.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted input is concatenated into a command string handed to a shell, so shell metacharacters such as semicolons, pipes, or command substitution let an attacker run additional commands.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The program is invoked directly with its arguments passed as a list, so the operating system never re-parses them, and no shell is involved unless an argument allowlist is enforced.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Process.Start("cmd.exe", "/c ping " + host);
```
Secure:
```
var psi = new ProcessStartInfo("ping");
psi.ArgumentList.Add("-n"); psi.ArgumentList.Add("1"); psi.ArgumentList.Add(host);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
run("ping " + host, shell=true)
```
Secure:
```
run(["ping", "-c", "1", host])      # argument vector, no shell
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Runtime.getRuntime().exec("ping " + host);
```
Secure:
```
new ProcessBuilder("ping", "-c", "1", host).start();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
child_process.exec("ping " + host);
```
Secure:
```
child_process.execFile("ping", ["-c", "1", host]);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
os.system("ping " + host)
```
Secure:
```
subprocess.run(["ping", "-c", "1", host])
```
embed_text:
The application must protect against OS command injection by using parameterized OS calls — passing arguments as a vector — or contextual command-line encoding, and must avoid invoking a shell.
Untrusted input is concatenated into a command string handed to a shell, so shell metacharacters such as semicolons, pipes, or command substitution let an attacker run additional commands.
The program is invoked directly with its arguments passed as a list, so the operating system never re-parses them, and no shell is involved unless an argument allowlist is enforced.

**Security Rule:** The application must protect against LDAP injection, or implement specific controls that prevent untrusted input from altering an LDAP search filter or distinguished name.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted input is concatenated into an LDAP filter or DN, so filter metacharacters such as parentheses, asterisks, or backslashes let an attacker broaden the query or bypass authentication checks.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Untrusted values are escaped with the LDAP filter or DN encoder, or bound through a templated search API, so they are always treated as literal values inside the filter.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var s = new DirectorySearcher("(uid=" + username + ")");
```
Secure:
```
var s = new DirectorySearcher("(uid=" + LdapEncoder.FilterEncode(username) + ")");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
filter = "(uid=" + username + ")"
```
Secure:
```
filter = "(uid=" + ldapFilterEncode(username) + ")"
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ctx.search(base, "(uid=" + username + ")", controls);
```
Secure:
```
ctx.search(base, "(uid={0})", new Object[]{username}, controls);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client.search(base, { filter: "(uid=" + username + ")" });
```
Secure:
```
client.search(base, { filter: "(uid=" + ldapEscape.filter`${username}` + ")" });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
conn.search(base, "(uid=" + username + ")")
```
Secure:
```
from ldap3.utils.conv import escape_filter_chars
conn.search(base, "(uid=" + escape_filter_chars(username) + ")")
```
embed_text:
The application must protect against LDAP injection, or implement specific controls that prevent untrusted input from altering an LDAP search filter or distinguished name.
Untrusted input is concatenated into an LDAP filter or DN, so filter metacharacters such as parentheses, asterisks, or backslashes let an attacker broaden the query or bypass authentication checks.
Untrusted values are escaped with the LDAP filter or DN encoder, or bound through a templated search API, so they are always treated as literal values inside the filter.

**Security Rule:** The application must protect against XPath injection by using query parameterization or precompiled XPath expressions.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted input is concatenated into an XPath expression, so quotes or XPath operators let an attacker change the selection and read nodes they should not reach.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The XPath expression is fixed and untrusted values are supplied through variable bindings or a precompiled expression with a variable resolver, never by string concatenation.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
nav.Select("/users/user[name='" + name + "']");
```
Secure:
```
var e = nav.Compile("/users/user[name=$name]");
e.SetContext(varContext);   // $name resolved from a context, not concatenated
nav.Select(e);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
expr = "/users/user[name='" + name + "']"
```
Secure:
```
expr = "/users/user[name=$name]"   # bind $name as a variable
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
xpath.evaluate("/users/user[name='" + name + "']", doc);
```
Secure:
```
xpath.setXPathVariableResolver(v -> name);
xpath.compile("/users/user[name=$name]").evaluate(doc);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
doc.evaluate("/users/user[name='" + name + "']", doc, null, t, null);
```
Secure:
```
doc.evaluate("/users/user[name=$name]", doc, resolver, t, null);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tree.xpath("/users/user[name='" + name + "']")
```
Secure:
```
tree.xpath("/users/user[name=$name]", name=name)
```
embed_text:
The application must protect against XPath injection by using query parameterization or precompiled XPath expressions.
Untrusted input is concatenated into an XPath expression, so quotes or XPath operators let an attacker change the selection and read nodes they should not reach.
The XPath expression is fixed and untrusted values are supplied through variable bindings or a precompiled expression with a variable resolver, never by string concatenation.

**Security Rule:** LaTeX processors must be configured securely — for example without the shell-escape flag — and restricted to an allowlist of commands so untrusted content cannot trigger LaTeX injection.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted content is compiled by a LaTeX engine with shell-escape enabled or with dangerous primitives available, letting an attacker run commands or read arbitrary files during compilation.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The LaTeX engine runs without shell-escape, in a restricted or sandboxed mode, and untrusted input is confined to an allowlist of safe commands and escaped before inclusion.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Process.Start("pdflatex", "--shell-escape " + userTex);
```
Secure:
```
Process.Start("pdflatex", "-no-shell-escape " + sanitizedTex);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
compileLatex(userDoc, flags="--shell-escape")
```
Secure:
```
compileLatex(escapeLatex(userDoc), flags="--no-shell-escape")
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
new ProcessBuilder("pdflatex", "--shell-escape", userTex).start();
```
Secure:
```
new ProcessBuilder("pdflatex", "-no-shell-escape", sanitizedTex).start();
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
subprocess.run(["pdflatex", "--shell-escape", user_tex])
```
Secure:
```
subprocess.run(["pdflatex", "-no-shell-escape", "-interaction=nonstopmode",
                sanitized_tex])
```
embed_text:
LaTeX processors must be configured securely — for example without the shell-escape flag — and restricted to an allowlist of commands so untrusted content cannot trigger LaTeX injection.
Untrusted content is compiled by a LaTeX engine with shell-escape enabled or with dangerous primitives available, letting an attacker run commands or read arbitrary files during compilation.
The LaTeX engine runs without shell-escape, in a restricted or sandboxed mode, and untrusted input is confined to an allowlist of safe commands and escaped before inclusion.

**Security Rule:** The application must escape special characters before placing untrusted input into a regular expression, so they are matched literally and not interpreted as regex metacharacters.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted input is inserted into a regular expression unescaped, so metacharacters change what the pattern matches and can break the intended check or trigger catastrophic backtracking.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Untrusted input destined for a regular expression is passed through a regex-quote or escape function so every character is treated as a literal.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Regex.IsMatch(text, userTerm);
```
Secure:
```
Regex.IsMatch(text, Regex.Escape(userTerm));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
pattern = compile("^" + userInput + "$")
```
Secure:
```
pattern = compile("^" + regexEscape(userInput) + "$")
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Pattern.compile(userTerm).matcher(text).find();
```
Secure:
```
Pattern.compile(Pattern.quote(userTerm)).matcher(text).find();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
new RegExp(userTerm).test(text);
```
Secure:
```
new RegExp(userTerm.replace(/[.*+?^${}()|[\]\\]/g, "\\$&")).test(text);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
re.search(user_term, text)
```
Secure:
```
re.search(re.escape(user_term), text)
```
embed_text:
The application must escape special characters before placing untrusted input into a regular expression, so they are matched literally and not interpreted as regex metacharacters.
Untrusted input is inserted into a regular expression unescaped, so metacharacters change what the pattern matches and can break the intended check or trigger catastrophic backtracking.
Untrusted input destined for a regular expression is passed through a regex-quote or escape function so every character is treated as a literal.

**Security Rule:** The application must protect against CSV and formula injection by following RFC 4180 escaping and prefixing any field that begins with a formula trigger character with a single quote when exporting.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** User-controlled values are written into exported CSV or spreadsheet cells unchanged, so a value starting with an equals, plus, minus, or at sign is executed as a formula when the file is opened.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each exported field is RFC 4180 quoted, and any value whose first character is a formula trigger is prefixed with a single quote so the spreadsheet treats it as inert text.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
sb.AppendLine(name + "," + note);
```
Secure:
```
string Safe(string v) => "=+-@".Contains(v.FirstOrDefault()) ? "'" + v : v;
sb.AppendLine(Csv(Safe(name)) + "," + Csv(Safe(note)));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
row = name + "," + note          # note may start with = + - or @
```
Secure:
```
row = csvQuote(name) + "," + csvQuote(neutralizeFormula(note))
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
writer.writeNext(new String[]{ name, note });
```
Secure:
```
String safe = note.matches("^[=+\\-@].*") ? "'" + note : note;
writer.writeNext(new String[]{ name, safe });
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
line = [name, note].join(",");
```
Secure:
```
const safe = v => /^[=+\-@]/.test(v) ? "'" + v : v;
line = [name, note].map(safe).map(csvQuote).join(",");
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
writer.writerow([name, note])
```
Secure:
```
def safe(v): return "'" + v if v[:1] in ("=", "+", "-", "@") else v
writer.writerow([safe(name), safe(note)])
```
embed_text:
The application must protect against CSV and formula injection by following RFC 4180 escaping and prefixing any field that begins with a formula trigger character with a single quote when exporting.
User-controlled values are written into exported CSV or spreadsheet cells unchanged, so a value starting with an equals, plus, minus, or at sign is executed as a formula when the file is opened.
Each exported field is RFC 4180 quoted, and any value whose first character is a formula trigger is prefixed with a single quote so the spreadsheet treats it as inert text.

**Security Rule:** Untrusted HTML from rich-text or WYSIWYG editors must be sanitized with a well-known, maintained HTML sanitization library before it is stored or rendered.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Rich-text HTML submitted by a user is stored and later rendered as raw markup, so embedded script or event-handler attributes execute in the browser of anyone who views it.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** User HTML is passed through a dedicated sanitizer that strips scripts, event handlers, and unsafe attributes, keeping only an allowlist of formatting tags before storage or rendering.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
div.InnerHtml = userHtml;
```
Secure:
```
div.InnerHtml = new HtmlSanitizer().Sanitize(userHtml);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
store(userHtml)            # rendered later as raw markup
```
Secure:
```
store(htmlSanitizer.clean(userHtml))
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
out.print(userHtml);
```
Secure:
```
out.print(htmlPolicy.sanitize(userHtml));   // OWASP Java HTML Sanitizer
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
el.innerHTML = userHtml;
```
Secure:
```
el.innerHTML = DOMPurify.sanitize(userHtml);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return Markup(request.form["bio"])
```
Secure:
```
import nh3
return Markup(nh3.clean(request.form["bio"]))
```
embed_text:
Untrusted HTML from rich-text or WYSIWYG editors must be sanitized with a well-known, maintained HTML sanitization library before it is stored or rendered.
Rich-text HTML submitted by a user is stored and later rendered as raw markup, so embedded script or event-handler attributes execute in the browser of anyone who views it.
User HTML is passed through a dedicated sanitizer that strips scripts, event handlers, and unsafe attributes, keeping only an allowlist of formatting tags before storage or rendering.

**Security Rule:** The application must avoid dynamic code execution features such as eval or Spring Expression Language, and where unavoidable must strictly sanitize any user input before it is executed.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted input is passed to a dynamic code or expression evaluator, letting an attacker run arbitrary code in the process.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Dynamic evaluation is replaced with a safe parser, a lookup table, or a literal evaluator, so untrusted input is interpreted as data and never as code.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var r = new DataTable().Compute(userInput, "");
```
Secure:
```
var r = allowedOps.TryGetValue(userInput, out var op) ? op() : null;
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

**Security Rule:** Data bound for a potentially dangerous context must be sanitized first — restricted to characters that are safe for that context and trimmed to a sane maximum length.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted data reaches a sensitive sink with no character allowlist and no length bound, so unexpected characters or oversized input drive injection or resource-exhaustion behaviour.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The value is reduced to an allowlist of context-safe characters and truncated to a maximum length before it is used.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var refId = Request.Query["ref"].ToString();
```
Secure:
```
var refId = Regex.Replace(Request.Query["ref"], "[^A-Za-z0-9_-]", "");
refId = refId[..Math.Min(refId.Length, 64)];
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
useInContext(rawValue)
```
Secure:
```
useInContext(allowlistFilter(rawValue) truncated to MAX_LEN)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String ref = request.getParameter("ref");
```
Secure:
```
String ref = request.getParameter("ref").replaceAll("[^A-Za-z0-9_-]", "");
ref = ref.substring(0, Math.min(ref.length(), 64));
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const ref = req.query.ref;
```
Secure:
```
const ref = req.query.ref.replace(/[^A-Za-z0-9_-]/g, "").slice(0, 64);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ref = request.args["ref"]
```
Secure:
```
ref = re.sub(r"[^A-Za-z0-9_-]", "", request.args["ref"])[:64]
```
embed_text:
Data bound for a potentially dangerous context must be sanitized first — restricted to characters that are safe for that context and trimmed to a sane maximum length.
Untrusted data reaches a sensitive sink with no character allowlist and no length bound, so unexpected characters or oversized input drive injection or resource-exhaustion behaviour.
The value is reduced to an allowlist of context-safe characters and truncated to a maximum length before it is used.

**Security Rule:** User-supplied SVG content must be validated or sanitized so it contains only tags and attributes that are safe for the application, excluding scripts and foreignObject.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An uploaded or pasted SVG is served or inlined unchanged, so script elements, event handlers, or a foreignObject inside it execute when the image is rendered.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The SVG is run through a sanitizer configured for the SVG profile that drops scripts, event handlers, and foreignObject, keeping only safe drawing tags and attributes.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return Content(uploadedSvg, "image/svg+xml");
```
Secure:
```
var s = new HtmlSanitizer { AllowedTags = { "svg", "path", "g", "rect" } };
return Content(s.Sanitize(uploadedSvg), "image/svg+xml");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
serve(uploadedSvg)
```
Secure:
```
serve(svgSanitizer.clean(uploadedSvg))   # scripts/foreignObject removed
```

Vulnerable:

out.write(uploadedSvg);
```
Secure:
```

```

Vulnerable:
```
container.innerHTML = uploadedSvg;
```
Secure:
```
container.innerHTML = DOMPurify.sanitize(uploadedSvg,
    { USE_PROFILES: { svg: true } });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return Response(uploaded_svg, mimetype="image/svg+xml")
```
Secure:
```
clean = nh3.clean(uploaded_svg, tags={"svg", "path", "g", "rect"})
return Response(clean, mimetype="image/svg+xml")
```

The SVG is run through a sanitizer configured for the SVG profile that drops scripts, event handlers, and foreignObject, keeping only safe drawing tags and attributes.

**Security Rule:** User-supplied scriptable or expression markup such as Markdown, CSS, XSL stylesheets, or BBCode must be sanitized, or its scriptable features disabled, before it is rendered.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Markup languages that can embed scripts, expressions, or remote references are rendered from untrusted input without restriction, so the embedded active content runs in the viewer context.

**Security Rule:** The application must protect against Server-side Request Forgery by validating untrusted URLs against an allowlist of protocols, domains, paths, and ports before using them to call another service.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A user-supplied URL or host is fetched server-side without restriction, so an attacker can reach internal services, the cloud metadata endpoint, or non-HTTP schemes.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The target URL is parsed and its scheme, host, and port are checked against an allowlist, and internal address ranges are rejected, before any server-side request is made.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var data = await httpClient.GetStringAsync(userUrl);
```
Secure:
```
var u = new Uri(userUrl);
if (AllowedHosts.Contains(u.Host)) await httpClient.GetStringAsync(u);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
response = httpGet(userUrl)
```
Secure:
```
if host(userUrl) in ALLOWED_HOSTS: response = httpGet(userUrl)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var conn = new URL(userUrl).openConnection();
```
Secure:
```
URL u = new URL(userUrl);
if (ALLOWED_HOSTS.contains(u.getHost())) u.openConnection();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const data = await fetch(req.query.url);
```
Secure:
```
const u = new URL(req.query.url);
if (ALLOWED_HOSTS.has(u.hostname)) await fetch(u);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
data = requests.get(request.args["url"]).text
```
Secure:
```
host = urlparse(request.args["url"]).hostname
if host in ALLOWED_HOSTS:
    data = requests.get(request.args["url"]).text
```

A user-supplied URL or host is fetched server-side without restriction, so an attacker can reach internal services, the cloud metadata endpoint, or non-HTTP schemes.
The target URL is parsed and its scheme, host, and port are checked against an allowlist, and internal address ranges are rejected, before any server-side request is made.

**Security Rule:** The application must not build templates from untrusted input, and where unavoidable any untrusted value included during template creation must be strictly validated or sanitized.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted input is concatenated into a template that is then compiled, so template-engine syntax in the input runs as code on the server.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The template text is fixed and trusted, and untrusted values are passed only as render-time data, never concatenated into the template source.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var result = Engine.Razor.RunCompile("Hi " + name, "k", null, null);
```
Secure:
```
var result = Engine.Razor.RunCompile("Hi @Model.Name", "k", null,
                                      new { Name = name });
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
render(compileTemplate("Hello " + userInput))
```
Secure:
```
render(compileTemplate("Hello {{ name }}"), name=userInput)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Template t = engine.createTemplate("Hi " + name);
```
Secure:
```
Template t = engine.getTemplate("greeting.ftl");  // fixed, name as model
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const out = ejs.render("Hi " + name);
```
Secure:
```
const out = ejs.render("Hi <%= name %>", { name });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Template("Hi " + request.args["name"]).render()
```
Secure:
```
Template("Hi {{ name }}").render(name=request.args["name"])
```

Untrusted input is concatenated into a template that is then compiled, so template-engine syntax in the input runs as code on the server.
The template text is fixed and trusted, and untrusted values are passed only as render-time data, never concatenated into the template source.

```

```
Secure:
```
if (ALLOWED_NAMES.contains(name))
    o = new InitialContext().lookup(name);   // remote codebase disabled
```

**Security Rule:** Content must be sanitized before it is sent to memcache so that control characters cannot inject additional cache commands.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted data is used as a memcache key or value with control characters or whitespace intact, so newline or space characters split the protocol stream into extra commands.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Keys and values are validated to reject whitespace and control characters, or are hashed or encoded, before the memcache call.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
cache.Store(StoreMode.Set, userKey, value);
```
Secure:
```
if (!Regex.IsMatch(userKey, "\\s"))
    cache.Store(StoreMode.Set, userKey, value);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
cache.set(userKey, userValue)
```
Secure:
```
if isClean(userKey): cache.set(userKey, encode(userValue))
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
memcachedClient.set(userKey, 0, value);
```
Secure:
```
if (!userKey.matches(".*\\s.*")) memcachedClient.set(userKey, 0, value);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
memcached.set(userKey, value, 0, cb);
```
Secure:
```
if (!/\s/.test(userKey)) memcached.set(userKey, value, 0, cb);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client.set(user_key, value)
```
Secure:
```

**Security Rule:** Format strings that could resolve in an unexpected or malicious way must be sanitized or fixed before they are processed.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An untrusted value is used as the format string itself, so format specifiers in the input read unintended arguments, crash the process, or in unmanaged code corrupt memory.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The format string is a fixed, trusted literal and untrusted values are passed only as arguments to it.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var s = string.Format(userFormat, value);
```
Secure:
```
var s = string.Format("{0}", userValue);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:
```
output = format("{}", userValue)        # fixed format, value as arg
```

Vulnerable:
```
String s = String.format(userFormat, value);
```
Secure:
```
String s = String.format("%s", userValue);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
msg = request.args["tpl"].format(config=CONFIG)
```
Secure:
```
msg = "Hello {name}".format(name=request.args["name"])
```

**Security Rule:** User input passed to mail systems must be sanitized to protect against SMTP and IMAP injection.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted input is placed into an email header such as the recipient or subject with carriage return or line feed characters intact, so an attacker injects extra headers or message commands.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Carriage return and line feed characters are stripped or rejected from any value that becomes part of an email header, ideally by using a mail library that validates headers.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
mail.Subject = Request.Form["subject"];
```
Secure:
```
mail.Subject = Request.Form["subject"].Replace("\r", "").Replace("\n", "");
```

header("To: " + userEmail)
```
Secure:
```
header("To: " + stripCrlf(userEmail))
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
message.setSubject(request.getParameter("subject"));
```
Secure:
```
message.setSubject(request.getParameter("subject")
    .replaceAll("[\\r\\n]", ""));
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
transport.sendMail({ to: userEmail, subject: userSubject });
```
Secure:
```
const clean = s => s.replace(/[\r\n]/g, "");
transport.sendMail({ to: clean(userEmail), subject: clean(userSubject) });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:

```

**Security Rule:** Regular expressions must be free of constructs that cause exponential backtracking, and untrusted input matched against them must be sanitized or bounded to mitigate ReDoS attacks.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A regular expression with nested or overlapping quantifiers is run against untrusted input, so a crafted string forces exponential backtracking and stalls the process.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The pattern is rewritten to a linear form, or matched with a non-backtracking engine, and input length is capped so worst-case matching time stays bounded.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Regex.IsMatch(userInput, "(a+)+$");
```
Secure:
```
new Regex("a+$", RegexOptions.NonBacktracking,
          TimeSpan.FromMilliseconds(100)).IsMatch(userInput);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
match("(a+)+$", untrustedInput)
```
Secure:
```
match("a+$", boundedLength(untrustedInput))    # linear, length-capped
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Pattern.compile("(a+)+").matcher(userInput).matches();
```
Secure:
```
Pattern.compile("a+").matcher(
    userInput.substring(0, Math.min(userInput.length(), 256))).matches();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
/^(\w+\s?)*$/.test(userInput);
```
Secure:
```
/^\w+(\s\w+)*$/.test(userInput.slice(0, 256));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
re.match(r"(\d+)+$", user_input)
```
Secure:
```
re.match(r"\d+$", user_input[:256])
```
embed_text:
Regular expressions must be free of constructs that cause exponential backtracking, and untrusted input matched against them must be sanitized or bounded to mitigate ReDoS attacks.
A regular expression with nested or overlapping quantifiers is run against untrusted input, so a crafted string forces exponential backtracking and stalls the process.
The pattern is rewritten to a linear form, or matched with a non-backtracking engine, and input length is capped so worst-case matching time stays bounded.

**Security Rule:** Applications using unmanaged code must use memory-safe string handling, bounded memory-copy routines, and safe pointer arithmetic to detect or prevent stack, buffer, and heap overflows.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Data is copied into a fixed-size buffer with a routine that does not take the destination size, so input longer than the buffer overflows adjacent memory.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Every copy uses a length-bounded routine tied to the destination capacity, or a memory-safe string type, so the write can never exceed the buffer.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
char buf[64];
strcpy(buf, userInput);          // no bound — overflows past 64 bytes
```
Secure:
```
char buf[64];
snprintf(buf, sizeof(buf), "%s", userInput);
```

Every copy uses a length-bounded routine tied to the destination capacity, or a memory-safe string type, so the write can never exceed the buffer.

**Security Rule:** Sign, range, and input validation techniques must be used to prevent integer overflows.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An arithmetic operation on untrusted integers is performed without a range check, so the result wraps around and produces a wrong size, index, or amount.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Operands are range-checked before the operation, or a checked arithmetic routine that traps on overflow is used.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
int total = count * price;
```
Secure:
```
int total = checked(count * price);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
size = count * itemSize       // wraps, then a short buffer is allocated
buf = allocate(size)
```
Secure:
```
if count > MAX / itemSize: reject()
buf = allocate(count * itemSize)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
int total = count * price;
```
Secure:
```
int total = Math.multiplyExact(count, price);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const total = a + b;          // a, b near Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER
```
Secure:
```
if (a > Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER - b) throw new Error("overflow");
const total = a + b;
```

An arithmetic operation on untrusted integers is performed without a range check, so the result wraps around and produces a wrong size, index, or amount.

**Security Rule:** Dynamically allocated memory and resources must be released, and references or pointers to freed memory must be cleared, to prevent dangling pointers and use-after-free vulnerabilities.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Memory is freed but the pointer to it is kept and used again, so the program reads or writes through a dangling pointer into reallocated memory.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The pointer is set to null immediately after the memory is freed, so any later use fails fast instead of touching reused memory.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
free(record);
log(record->name);          // use-after-free
```
Secure:
```
free(record);
record = NULL;              // later use fails fast
```

Dynamically allocated memory and resources must be released, and references or pointers to freed memory must be cleared, to prevent dangling pointers and use-after-free vulnerabilities.
Memory is freed but the pointer to it is kept and used again, so the program reads or writes through a dangling pointer into reallocated memory.
The pointer is set to null immediately after the memory is freed, so any later use fails fast instead of touching reused memory.

**Security Rule:** XML parsers must use a restrictive configuration with unsafe features such as external entity resolution disabled, to prevent XML External Entity attacks.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An XML parser is used with its default configuration, so a document that declares an external entity causes the parser to read local files or make network requests.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The parser is configured to disallow document type definitions and external entities, or a hardened XML library is used, before any untrusted XML is parsed.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var doc = new XmlDocument();
doc.LoadXml(untrustedXml);
```
Secure:
```
var doc = new XmlDocument { XmlResolver = null };
doc.LoadXml(untrustedXml);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
doc = xmlParser.parse(untrustedXml)        # entities resolved
```
Secure:
```
xmlParser.disableExternalEntities()
doc = xmlParser.parse(untrustedXml)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
DocumentBuilderFactory dbf = DocumentBuilderFactory.newInstance();
```
Secure:
```
DocumentBuilderFactory dbf = DocumentBuilderFactory.newInstance();
dbf.setFeature("http://apache.org/xml/features/disallow-doctype-decl", true);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const doc = new DOMParser().parseFromString(xml, "application/xml");
```
Secure:
```
const doc = parseXml(xml, { resolveExternalEntities: false, dtd: false });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]

```

```

**Security Rule:** Deserialization of untrusted data must enforce safe input handling such as an allowlist of object types, and mechanisms that are known to be insecure must not be used with untrusted input.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted bytes are deserialized by a mechanism that can instantiate arbitrary types, so a crafted payload builds a gadget chain that runs code during deserialization.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Untrusted data is parsed with a data-only format such as JSON into a known target type, or deserialization is restricted to an allowlist of expected classes.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var o = new BinaryFormatter().Deserialize(stream);
```
Secure:
```
var o = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<KnownDto>(stream);
```

Vulnerable:

obj = nativeDeserialize(untrustedBytes)
```
Secure:

obj = jsonParse(untrustedBytes, intoType=KnownDto)
```

embed_text:
Deserialization of untrusted data must enforce safe input handling such as an allowlist of object types, and mechanisms that are known to be insecure must not be used with untrusted input.
Untrusted bytes are deserialized by a mechanism that can instantiate arbitrary types, so a crafted payload builds a gadget chain that runs code during deserialization.
Untrusted data is parsed with a data-only format such as JSON into a known target type, or deserialization is restricted to an allowlist of expected classes.

**Security Rule:** When several parsers handle the same data type, they must parse consistently and use the same character encoding, so a value cannot be validated by one parser and interpreted differently by another.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** One parser validates a value and a different parser later interprets the same bytes, so a payload that looks safe to the validator resolves to a dangerous value at the sink.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A single canonical parser, with one fixed encoding, is used for both validation and use of a given data type, so what is checked is exactly what is consumed.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if validate(strictParse(data)): use(lenientParse(data))
```
Secure:
```
parsed = canonicalParse(data)
if validate(parsed): use(parsed)
```

Vulnerable:

    forward(gson.fromJson(body, Map.class));
```
Secure:
```

if (isSafe(parsed)) forward(parsed);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (isSafe(JSON.parse(body))) downstream(customParse(body));
```
Secure:
```
const parsed = JSON.parse(body);
if (isSafe(parsed)) downstream(parsed);
```

    forward_to_service(simplejson.loads(body))
```
Secure:

if is_safe(parsed): forward_to_service(parsed)
```

**Security Rule:** Access and refresh tokens must only be exposed to components that strictly need them. With a backend-for-frontend pattern, browser JavaScript must never hold tokens — they stay confined to the backend.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Access or refresh tokens are returned to the browser and stored in localStorage, sessionStorage, or a script-readable cookie, so any cross-site scripting flaw or third-party script can exfiltrate them.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Tokens are held only by a confidential backend; the browser receives just an opaque session cookie marked HttpOnly and Secure, and the backend attaches the real token to upstream calls on the user's behalf.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return Ok(new { access_token = token, refresh_token = refresh });
```
Secure:
```
HttpContext.Session.SetString("tokenRef", _vault.Store(token, refresh));
return Redirect("/app");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
respondToBrowser({ access_token: token, refresh_token: refresh })
```
Secure:
```
storeTokensServerSide(session, token, refresh)
setCookie("sid", session.id, httpOnly=true, secure=true)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
model.addAttribute("accessToken", tokens.getAccessToken());
```
Secure:
```
httpSession.setAttribute("tokenRef", tokenStore.save(tokens));
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
localStorage.setItem("access_token", resp.access_token);
```
Secure:
```
// BFF keeps tokens; browser calls same-origin API with the session cookie
await fetch("/api/profile", { credentials: "same-origin" });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return JSONResponse({"access_token": token, "refresh_token": refresh})
```
Secure:
```
request.session["token_ref"] = vault.store(token, refresh)
return RedirectResponse("/app")  # browser only gets the session cookie
```
embed_text:
Access and refresh tokens must only be exposed to components that strictly need them. With a backend-for-frontend pattern, browser JavaScript must never hold tokens — they stay confined to the backend.
Access or refresh tokens are returned to the browser and stored in localStorage, sessionStorage, or a script-readable cookie, so any cross-site scripting flaw or third-party script can exfiltrate them.
Tokens are held only by a confidential backend; the browser receives just an opaque session cookie marked HttpOnly and Secure, and the backend attaches the real token to upstream calls on the user's behalf.

**Security Rule:** The client must accept authorization-server values such as the code or ID Token only when they belong to a flow it started, by binding unguessable per-transaction secrets (PKCE code_verifier, state, nonce) to the same user-agent session.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The client omits or does not check the state, nonce, or PKCE binding, so an attacker can inject an authorization response from a flow the victim never initiated and have it accepted.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The client generates a random per-transaction state, nonce, and PKCE verifier, stores them bound to the user-agent session, and on callback rejects the response unless the returned values match what was stored.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var token = await _client.ExchangeCodeAsync(Request.Query["code"]);
```
Secure:
```
if (Request.Query["state"] != HttpContext.Session.GetString("state"))
    return BadRequest();
var token = await _client.ExchangeCodeAsync(code, verifier);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
onCallback(code): exchangeCodeForToken(code)
```
Secure:
```
onCallback(code, state):
  assert state == session.state and pkceMatches(session.verifier)
  exchangeCodeForToken(code, session.verifier)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
OAuth2Token token = client.exchange(request.getParameter("code"));
```
Secure:
```
if (!request.getParameter("state").equals(session.getAttribute("state")))
    throw new SecurityException("state mismatch");
OAuth2Token token = client.exchange(code, (String) session.getAttribute("verifier"));
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const token = await client.exchange({ code: params.get("code") });
```
Secure:
```
if (params.get("state") !== sessionStorage.getItem("oauth_state")) throw Error("bad state");
const token = await client.exchange({ code: params.get("code"), codeVerifier: stored });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
token = oauth.fetch_token(code=request.args["code"])
```
Secure:
```
if request.args["state"] != session.pop("oauth_state"):
    abort(400)
token = oauth.fetch_token(code=request.args["code"],
                          code_verifier=session.pop("pkce_verifier"))
```
embed_text:
The client must accept authorization-server values such as the code or ID Token only when they belong to a flow it started, by binding unguessable per-transaction secrets (PKCE code_verifier, state, nonce) to the same user-agent session.
The client omits or does not check the state, nonce, or PKCE binding, so an attacker can inject an authorization response from a flow the victim never initiated and have it accepted.
The client generates a random per-transaction state, nonce, and PKCE verifier, stores them bound to the user-agent session, and on callback rejects the response unless the returned values match what was stored.

A code-flow OAuth client must defend its token request against cross-site request forgery, either with PKCE or by validating the state parameter returned in the authorization response.
The callback exchanges any code it receives for tokens without checking PKCE or a state value, so an attacker can forge a callback request and have the victim's client log in under the attacker's code.
The client stores a random state value (and a PKCE verifier) before redirecting, and the callback handler rejects the request unless the returned state matches and the verifier completes the PKCE exchange.

**Security Rule:** An OAuth client that can talk to more than one authorization server must defend against mix-up attacks, for example by requiring the iss parameter and validating it on both the authorization and token responses.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The client tracks only that a flow is in progress, not which authorization server it was started with, so an attacker can swap responses between servers and have a code redeemed at the wrong server.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The client records which authorization server each transaction targets and verifies the iss value returned in the authorization response matches that server before redeeming the code at its token endpoint.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var token = await _client.Exchange(tokenEndpoint, code);
```
Secure:
```
if (iss != HttpContext.Session.GetString("expectedIss"))
    throw new SecurityException("mix-up attack");
var token = await _client.Exchange(EndpointFor(iss), code);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
onCallback(code): redeemAt(defaultTokenEndpoint, code)
```
Secure:
```
onCallback(code, iss):
  if iss != session.expectedIssuer: reject()
  redeemAt(issuerToEndpoint(iss), code)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Token t = client.exchange(tokenEndpoint, code);
```
Secure:
```
if (!iss.equals(session.getAttribute("expectedIss")))
    throw new SecurityException("mix-up attack");
Token t = client.exchange(endpointFor(iss), code);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const token = await exchange(tokenEndpoint, req.query.code);
```
Secure:
```
if (req.query.iss !== req.session.expectedIss) throw Error("mix-up");
const token = await exchange(endpointFor(req.query.iss), req.query.code);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
token = exchange(token_endpoint, request.args["code"])
```
Secure:
```
if request.args["iss"] != session["expected_iss"]:
    abort(400, "mix-up attack")
token = exchange(endpoint_for(request.args["iss"]), request.args["code"])
```
embed_text:
An OAuth client that can talk to more than one authorization server must defend against mix-up attacks, for example by requiring the iss parameter and validating it on both the authorization and token responses.
The client tracks only that a flow is in progress, not which authorization server it was started with, so an attacker can swap responses between servers and have a code redeemed at the wrong server.
The client records which authorization server each transaction targets and verifies the iss value returned in the authorization response matches that server before redeeming the code at its token endpoint.

**Security Rule:** The OAuth client must request only the scopes and authorization parameters it actually needs from the authorization server, following the principle of least privilege.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The client requests broad or wildcard scopes far beyond what the feature uses, so a compromised token or client grants the attacker access to data and operations the application never needed.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The authorization request lists only the minimal scopes required for the current operation, keeping the issued token's privileges as narrow as the feature allows.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
options.Scope.Add("openid"); options.Scope.Add("profile"); options.Scope.Add("admin");
```
Secure:
```
options.Scope.Add("openid"); options.Scope.Add("profile");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
authorizeUrl(scope="openid profile email admin offline_access all")
```
Secure:
```
authorizeUrl(scope="openid profile")   # only what this feature needs
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
request.setScope("read write admin offline_access");
```
Secure:
```
request.setScope("read");
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const url = client.authorizeUrl({ scope: "openid profile email admin" });
```
Secure:
```
const url = client.authorizeUrl({ scope: "openid profile" });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:

```

**Security Rule:** The resource server must accept an access token only when that token is intended for it, checking the audience via the aud claim of a structured token or through the token introspection endpoint.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The resource server validates only that a token's signature is good, so a token minted for a different service is accepted and can be replayed across APIs that share an issuer.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** After signature validation the resource server checks that the token's audience equals its own identifier, rejecting any token whose aud claim or introspection result names a different service.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var p = handler.ValidateToken(token, new TokenValidationParameters {
  ValidateAudience = false }, out _);
```
Secure:
```
var p = handler.ValidateToken(token, new TokenValidationParameters {
  ValidateAudience = true, ValidAudience = "https://api.myservice.example" }, out _);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
claims = verifySignature(token); allow(claims)
```
Secure:
```
claims = verifySignature(token)
if claims.aud != "https://api.myservice.example": reject()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Jws<Claims> jws = parser.parseClaimsJws(token);
```
Secure:
```
Jws<Claims> jws = Jwts.parser().requireAudience("https://api.myservice.example")
    .verifyWith(key).build().parseSignedClaims(token);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const claims = jwt.verify(token, key, { algorithms: ["RS256"] });
```
Secure:
```
const claims = jwt.verify(token, key,
  { algorithms: ["RS256"], audience: "https://api.myservice.example" });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
claims = jwt.decode(token, key, algorithms=["RS256"])
```
Secure:
```
claims = jwt.decode(token, key, algorithms=["RS256"],
                    audience="https://api.myservice.example")
```

The resource server must accept an access token only when that token is intended for it, checking the audience via the aud claim of a structured token or through the token introspection endpoint.
The resource server validates only that a token's signature is good, so a token minted for a different service is accepted and can be replayed across APIs that share an issuer.
After signature validation the resource server checks that the token's audience equals its own identifier, rejecting any token whose aud claim or introspection result names a different service.

**Security Rule:** The resource server must base authorization decisions on the delegated authorization claims of the access token, including sub, scope, and authorization_details when those claims are present.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The resource server treats any validly signed token as full access and ignores the scope or authorization_details claims, so a token issued for a narrow purpose can perform privileged operations.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each protected operation checks that the token carries the required scope or authorization_details entry, denying the request when the delegated authorization does not cover the requested action.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
[Authorize] public IActionResult Delete(int id) { _repo.Delete(id); return Ok(); }
```
Secure:
```
[Authorize(Policy = "records:delete")]
public IActionResult Delete(int id) { _repo.Delete(id); return Ok(); }
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if validToken(token): performWrite()
```
Secure:
```
if "write" in claims.scope: performWrite()
else: deny()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (jws != null) repository.delete(id);
```
Secure:
```
if (!claims.get("scope", String.class).contains("records:delete"))
    throw new ForbiddenException();
repository.delete(id);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (verified) deleteRecord(id);
```
Secure:
```
if (!claims.scope.split(" ").includes("records:delete")) return res.sendStatus(403);
deleteRecord(id);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if token_is_valid(token):
    delete_record(id)
```
Secure:
```
if "records:delete" not in claims["scope"].split():
    abort(403)
delete_record(id)
```

The resource server treats any validly signed token as full access and ignores the scope or authorization_details claims, so a token issued for a narrow purpose can perform privileged operations.
Each protected operation checks that the token carries the required scope or authorization_details entry, denying the request when the delegated authorization does not cover the requested action.

document:
**Security Rule:** When an access-control decision needs a unique user, the resource server must identify that user from claims that cannot be reassigned, typically the combination of iss and sub rather than a mutable claim.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The resource server keys the user on a reassignable claim such as email or preferred_username, so when that value is changed or reused a new account silently inherits another user's data and permissions.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The resource server derives the user key from the immutable iss plus sub pair, so the local account binding survives email changes and cannot be hijacked by reusing a freed username.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var user = _db.Users.SingleOrDefault(u => u.Email == claims["email"]);
```
Secure:
```
var key = $"{claims["iss"]}|{claims["sub"]}";
var user = _db.Users.SingleOrDefault(u => u.SubjectKey == key);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
user = lookupByEmail(claims.email)
```
Secure:
```
user = lookupBySubject(claims.iss + "|" + claims.sub)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
User u = userRepo.findByEmail(claims.get("email", String.class));
```
Secure:
```
String key = claims.getIssuer() + "|" + claims.getSubject();
User u = userRepo.findBySubjectKey(key);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const user = await db.users.findOne({ email: claims.email });
```
Secure:
```
const user = await db.users.findOne({ subjectKey: `${claims.iss}|${claims.sub}` });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
user = User.query.filter_by(email=claims["email"]).first()
```
Secure:
```
key = f"{claims['iss']}|{claims['sub']}"
user = User.query.filter_by(subject_key=key).first()
```

The resource server keys the user on a reassignable claim such as email or preferred_username, so when that value is changed or reused a new account silently inherits another user's data and permissions.
The resource server derives the user key from the immutable iss plus sub pair, so the local account binding survives email changes and cannot be hijacked by reusing a freed username.

**Security Rule:** When a resource server requires a specific authentication strength, method, or recentness, it must verify the access token satisfies those constraints using the OIDC acr, amr, and auth_time claims.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A sensitive operation accepts any valid token regardless of how the user authenticated, so a session established with a weak factor or long ago is enough to perform a high-assurance action.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Before a sensitive operation the resource server checks the acr or amr claim meets the required assurance level and that auth_time is recent enough, prompting step-up authentication otherwise.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (User.Identity.IsAuthenticated) _bank.Transfer(amount);
```
Secure:
```
if (User.FindFirstValue("acr") != "mfa" ||
    DateTimeOffset.UtcNow.ToUnixTimeSeconds() - authTime > 300)
  return Unauthorized("step-up required");
_bank.Transfer(amount);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if validToken(token): doSensitiveAction()
```
Secure:
```
if claims.acr == "mfa" and now - claims.auth_time < 300:
  doSensitiveAction()
else: requireStepUp()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (jws != null) accountService.transfer(amount);
```
Secure:
```
if (!"mfa".equals(claims.get("acr")) ||
    Instant.now().getEpochSecond() - claims.get("auth_time", Long.class) > 300)
  throw new StepUpRequiredException();
accountService.transfer(amount);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (verified) transferFunds(amount);
```
Secure:
```
if (claims.acr !== "mfa" || Date.now() / 1000 - claims.auth_time > 300)
  return res.status(401).send("step-up required");
transferFunds(amount);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if token_valid:
    transfer_funds(amount)
```
Secure:
```
if claims.get("acr") != "mfa" or time.time() - claims["auth_time"] > 300:
    abort(401, "step-up required")
transfer_funds(amount)
```

document:
**Security Rule:** The resource server must prevent use or replay of stolen access tokens by requiring sender-constrained tokens, either Mutual TLS for OAuth 2 or OAuth 2 Demonstration of Proof of Possession.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The resource server accepts bearer access tokens with no proof of possession, so a token captured from logs, a proxy, or a compromised client can be replayed by any party that holds the string.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The resource server requires sender-constrained tokens and verifies the presenter holds the bound key — checking the mTLS client certificate thumbprint or a valid DPoP proof against the token's cnf claim.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var claims = handler.ValidateToken(token, parms, out _);
```
Secure:
```
var claims = handler.ValidateToken(token, parms, out _);
if (claims.FindFirstValue("cnf") != DpopThumbprint(Request.Headers["DPoP"]))
    return Unauthorized("DPoP mismatch");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if validToken(bearerToken): allow()
```
Secure:
```
if validToken(token) and dpopProofMatches(token.cnf, request.dpop):
  allow()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Claims c = parser.parseClaimsJws(token).getBody();
```
Secure:
```
Claims c = parser.parseClaimsJws(token).getBody();
if (!cnfThumbprint(c).equals(dpopThumbprint(request.getHeader("DPoP"))))
    throw new UnauthorizedException("DPoP mismatch");
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const claims = jwt.verify(token, key);
```
Secure:
```
const claims = jwt.verify(token, key);
if (claims.cnf.jkt !== dpopThumbprint(req.headers.dpop))
  return res.status(401).send("DPoP mismatch");
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
claims = jwt.decode(token, key, algorithms=["RS256"])
```
Secure:
```
claims = jwt.decode(token, key, algorithms=["RS256"])
if claims["cnf"]["jkt"] != dpop_thumbprint(request.headers["DPoP"]):
    abort(401, "DPoP proof mismatch")
```
embed_text:
The resource server must prevent use or replay of stolen access tokens by requiring sender-constrained tokens, either Mutual TLS for OAuth 2 or OAuth 2 Demonstration of Proof of Possession.
The resource server accepts bearer access tokens with no proof of possession, so a token captured from logs, a proxy, or a compromised client can be replayed by any party that holds the string.
The resource server requires sender-constrained tokens and verifies the presenter holds the bound key — checking the mTLS client certificate thumbprint or a valid DPoP proof against the token's cnf claim.

**Security Rule:** The authorization server must validate every redirect URI against a client-specific allowlist of pre-registered URIs using exact string comparison, never a prefix or pattern match.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The redirect URI is accepted on a substring, prefix, or wildcard match, so an attacker registers a lookalike path or open-redirect suffix and has the authorization code delivered to their endpoint.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The submitted redirect URI must be byte-for-byte equal to one of the URIs pre-registered for that client; any non-exact value is rejected before the authorization request proceeds.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (redirectUri.StartsWith(client.BaseUrl)) IssueCode();
```
Secure:
```
if (!client.RegisteredRedirectUris.Contains(redirectUri, StringComparer.Ordinal))
  return BadRequest("invalid redirect_uri");
IssueCode();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if redirectUri.startsWith(client.baseUrl): proceed()
```
Secure:
```
if redirectUri in client.registeredRedirectUris: proceed()
else: reject()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (redirectUri.startsWith(client.getBaseUrl())) issueCode();
```
Secure:
```
if (!client.getRegisteredRedirectUris().contains(redirectUri))
    throw new InvalidRequestException("invalid redirect_uri");
issueCode();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (redirectUri.includes(client.domain)) issueCode();
```
Secure:
```
if (!client.registeredRedirectUris.includes(redirectUri))
  return res.status(400).send("invalid redirect_uri");
issueCode();
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if redirect_uri.startswith(client.base_url):
    issue_code()
```
Secure:
```
if redirect_uri not in client.registered_redirect_uris:
    abort(400, "invalid redirect_uri")
issue_code()
```
embed_text:
The authorization server must validate every redirect URI against a client-specific allowlist of pre-registered URIs using exact string comparison, never a prefix or pattern match.
The redirect URI is accepted on a substring, prefix, or wildcard match, so an attacker registers a lookalike path or open-redirect suffix and has the authorization code delivered to their endpoint.
The submitted redirect URI must be byte-for-byte equal to one of the URIs pre-registered for that client; any non-exact value is rejected before the authorization request proceeds.

**Security Rule:** An authorization code must be usable only once; on a second token request with an already-redeemed code the authorization server must reject it and revoke any tokens already issued from that code.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The authorization server does not mark codes as consumed, so a code leaked through logs or the browser history can be replayed to mint a second set of tokens for the attacker.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Code redemption is atomic and single-use: the first valid token request consumes the code, and any later use of the same code is rejected and triggers revocation of the tokens issued from it.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var code = _codes.Find(codeValue);
return IssueTokens(code);
```
Secure:
```
var code = _codes.ConsumeOnce(codeValue);  // atomic mark-used
if (code is null) { _tokens.RevokeFor(codeValue); throw new InvalidGrantException(); }
return IssueTokens(code);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
code = lookup(codeValue); issueTokens(code)
```
Secure:
```
code = consumeOnce(codeValue)
if code.alreadyUsed: revokeTokensFor(code); reject()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
AuthCode code = codeRepo.find(codeValue);
return issueTokens(code);
```
Secure:
```
AuthCode code = codeRepo.findAndMarkUsed(codeValue);  // atomic
if (code == null) { revokeTokensFor(codeValue); throw new InvalidGrantException(); }
return issueTokens(code);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const code = await codes.find(codeValue);
return issueTokens(code);
```
Secure:
```
const code = await codes.findOneAndUpdate(
  { value: codeValue, used: false }, { used: true });
if (!code) { await revokeTokensFor(codeValue); throw Error("code reuse"); }
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
code = AuthCode.query.get(code_value)
return issue_tokens(code)
```
Secure:
```
code = AuthCode.query.with_for_update().get(code_value)
if code.used:
    revoke_tokens_for(code); abort(400, "code reuse")
code.used = True
return issue_tokens(code)
```
embed_text:
An authorization code must be usable only once; on a second token request with an already-redeemed code the authorization server must reject it and revoke any tokens already issued from that code.
The authorization server does not mark codes as consumed, so a code leaked through logs or the browser history can be replayed to mint a second set of tokens for the attacker.
Code redemption is atomic and single-use: the first valid token request consumes the code, and any later use of the same code is rejected and triggers revocation of the tokens issued from it.

**Security Rule:** Authorization codes must be short-lived — at most 10 minutes for L1 and L2 applications and at most 1 minute for L3 — and rejected once that lifetime has elapsed.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Authorization codes have a long or unbounded lifetime, so a code leaked through a referrer header or proxy log remains redeemable for hours, widening the window for replay.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each code carries a short expiry stamped at issue time, and the token endpoint rejects the code as expired once that brief window — minutes, not hours — has passed.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
code.ExpiresAt = DateTime.UtcNow.AddHours(24);
```
Secure:
```
code.ExpiresAt = DateTime.UtcNow.AddMinutes(1);
if (DateTime.UtcNow > code.ExpiresAt) throw new InvalidGrantException();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
code.expiresAt = now + hours(24)
```
Secure:
```
code.expiresAt = now + minutes(1)
if now > code.expiresAt: reject()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
code.setExpiresAt(Instant.now().plus(Duration.ofHours(24)));
```
Secure:
```
code.setExpiresAt(Instant.now().plus(Duration.ofMinutes(1)));
if (Instant.now().isAfter(code.getExpiresAt())) throw new InvalidGrantException();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
code.expiresAt = Date.now() + 24 * 3600 * 1000;
```
Secure:
```
code.expiresAt = Date.now() + 60 * 1000;
if (Date.now() > code.expiresAt) throw Error("code expired");
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
code.expires_at = datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(hours=24)
```
Secure:
```
code.expires_at = datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(minutes=1)
if datetime.utcnow() > code.expires_at:
    abort(400, "code expired")
```
embed_text:
Authorization codes must be short-lived — at most 10 minutes for L1 and L2 applications and at most 1 minute for L3 — and rejected once that lifetime has elapsed.
Authorization codes have a long or unbounded lifetime, so a code leaked through a referrer header or proxy log remains redeemable for hours, widening the window for replay.
Each code carries a short expiry stamped at issue time, and the token endpoint rejects the code as expired once that brief window — minutes, not hours — has passed.

**Security Rule:** For each client, the authorization server must allow only the grant types that client genuinely needs; the implicit token grant and the resource-owner password grant must no longer be used.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A client is configured to allow every grant type, including the deprecated implicit and password grants, so a single misuse or compromise exposes flows the application never relies on.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each client registration enumerates the minimal set of grant types it requires, and the authorization server rejects any grant request that is not on that per-client list; implicit and password are never enabled.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client.AllowedGrantTypes = GrantTypes.ImplicitAndClientCredentials;
```
Secure:
```
client.AllowedGrantTypes = GrantTypes.Code;
if (!client.AllowedGrantTypes.Contains(grantType)) throw new InvalidGrantException();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client.allowedGrants = "authorization_code token password refresh_token"
```
Secure:
```
client.allowedGrants = "authorization_code refresh_token"
if requestedGrant not in client.allowedGrants: reject()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client.setGrantTypes(List.of("authorization_code", "implicit", "password"));
```
Secure:
```
client.setGrantTypes(List.of("authorization_code", "refresh_token"));
if (!client.getGrantTypes().contains(grantType))
    throw new UnauthorizedGrantException();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client.grantTypes = ["authorization_code", "implicit", "password"];
```
Secure:
```
client.grantTypes = ["authorization_code", "refresh_token"];
if (!client.grantTypes.includes(grantType)) throw Error("unauthorized_grant_type");
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client.grant_types = ["authorization_code", "token", "password"]
```
Secure:
```
client.grant_types = ["authorization_code", "refresh_token"]
if grant_type not in client.grant_types:
    abort(400, "unauthorized_grant_type")
```
embed_text:
For each client, the authorization server must allow only the grant types that client genuinely needs; the implicit token grant and the resource-owner password grant must no longer be used.
A client is configured to allow every grant type, including the deprecated implicit and password grants, so a single misuse or compromise exposes flows the application never relies on.
Each client registration enumerates the minimal set of grant types it requires, and the authorization server rejects any grant request that is not on that per-client list; implicit and password are never enabled.

The authorization server must mitigate refresh-token replay for public clients, preferably with sender-constrained tokens; if rotation is used instead, it must invalidate each refresh token on use and revoke the whole chain if an already-used token is presented.
A public client's refresh token is long-lived and reusable, so a stolen refresh token can be redeemed repeatedly with no detection of the legitimate client and the attacker running in parallel.
Refresh tokens are sender-constrained, or rotated so each use returns a new token and invalidates the old one; presenting a consumed token triggers revocation of every refresh token in that authorization chain.
