**Security Rule:** For the code grant, the authorization server must require PKCE — a valid code_challenge with method S256, never plain — at the authorization endpoint, and must validate the code_verifier at the token endpoint.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The authorization server does not enforce PKCE or accepts the plain challenge method, so an attacker who intercepts an authorization code can redeem it without proving possession of the verifier.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The authorization endpoint rejects requests lacking a code_challenge or using method plain, and the token endpoint recomputes the S256 hash of the code_verifier and rejects any mismatch.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return IssueCode(clientId, redirectUri);
```
Secure:
```
if (codeChallengeMethod != "S256")
  return BadRequest("S256 PKCE required");
return IssueCode(clientId, redirectUri, codeChallenge);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
issueCode(req)   # no code_challenge required
```
Secure:
```
if req.code_challenge_method != "S256": reject()
if sha256(token_req.verifier) != storedChallenge: reject()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return issueCode(clientId, redirectUri);
```
Secure:
```
if (!"S256".equals(challengeMethod))
    throw new InvalidRequestException("S256 PKCE required");
return issueCode(clientId, redirectUri, codeChallenge);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
issueCode(req.query.client_id, req.query.redirect_uri);
```
Secure:
```
if (req.query.code_challenge_method !== "S256")
  return res.status(400).send("S256 PKCE required");
issueCode(req.query.client_id, req.query.redirect_uri, req.query.code_challenge);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return issue_code(client_id, redirect_uri)
```
Secure:
```
if request.args.get("code_challenge_method") != "S256":
    abort(400, "S256 PKCE required")
return issue_code(client_id, redirect_uri, code_challenge)
```
embed_text:
For the code grant, the authorization server must require PKCE — a valid code_challenge with method S256, never plain — at the authorization endpoint, and must validate the code_verifier at the token endpoint.
The authorization server does not enforce PKCE or accepts the plain challenge method, so an attacker who intercepts an authorization code can redeem it without proving possession of the verifier.
The authorization endpoint rejects requests lacking a code_challenge or using method plain, and the token endpoint recomputes the S256 hash of the code_verifier and rejects any mismatch.

**Security Rule:** If unauthenticated dynamic client registration is supported, the authorization server must validate the submitted client metadata, ensure user consent, and warn the user before processing an authorization request from an untrusted client.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Dynamic client registration accepts arbitrary metadata and the resulting client is treated as trusted, so an attacker self-registers a lookalike app and silently harvests user authorizations.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Dynamically registered client metadata such as redirect URIs and logos is validated, the client is flagged as untrusted, and the consent screen explicitly warns the user before any authorization proceeds.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var client = _clients.Register(request.Metadata);
```
Secure:
```
var meta = _validator.Validate(request.Metadata);
var client = _clients.Register(meta, trusted: false);
_consent.Show(client, warnUntrusted: true);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client = registerClient(submittedMetadata)
```
Secure:
```
client = registerClient(validate(submittedMetadata))
client.trusted = false
showConsentWithUntrustedWarning(client)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Client client = clientService.register(request.getMetadata());
```
Secure:
```
ClientMetadata meta = metadataValidator.validate(request.getMetadata());
Client client = clientService.register(meta, false);  // trusted=false
consentService.show(client, true);  // warn untrusted
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const client = await Client.create(req.body);
```
Secure:
```
const meta = validateClientMetadata(req.body);
const client = await Client.create({ ...meta, trusted: false });
renderConsent(client, { warnUntrusted: true });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client = Client.create(**request.json)
```
Secure:
```
meta = validate_client_metadata(request.json)
client = Client.create(**meta, trusted=False)
consent_screen(client, warn_untrusted=True)
```
embed_text:
If unauthenticated dynamic client registration is supported, the authorization server must validate the submitted client metadata, ensure user consent, and warn the user before processing an authorization request from an untrusted client.
Dynamic client registration accepts arbitrary metadata and the resulting client is treated as trusted, so an attacker self-registers a lookalike app and silently harvests user authorizations.
Dynamically registered client metadata such as redirect URIs and logos is validated, the client is flagged as untrusted, and the consent screen explicitly warns the user before any authorization proceeds.

**Security Rule:** Refresh tokens must have an absolute expiration, even when sliding expiration is applied, so the token cannot be renewed indefinitely.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A refresh token with sliding expiration is renewed on every use with no absolute cap, so a stolen token can be kept alive forever as long as the attacker keeps redeeming it.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The refresh token records an absolute maximum lifetime fixed at issue time; sliding renewal extends the window only up to that hard cap, after which the token is rejected and re-authentication is required.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
rt.ExpiresAt = DateTime.UtcNow.AddDays(7);
```
Secure:
```
var absolute = rt.IssuedAt.AddDays(30);
var sliding = DateTime.UtcNow.AddDays(7);
rt.ExpiresAt = sliding < absolute ? sliding : absolute;
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
rt.expiresAt = now + slidingWindow   # extended every use, no cap
```
Secure:
```
rt.absoluteExpiry = issuedAt + days(30)
rt.expiresAt = min(now + slidingWindow, rt.absoluteExpiry)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
rt.setExpiresAt(Instant.now().plus(Duration.ofDays(7)));
```
Secure:
```
Instant absolute = rt.getIssuedAt().plus(Duration.ofDays(30));
Instant sliding = Instant.now().plus(Duration.ofDays(7));
rt.setExpiresAt(sliding.isBefore(absolute) ? sliding : absolute);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
rt.expiresAt = Date.now() + 7 * 86400000;
```
Secure:
```
const absolute = rt.issuedAt + 30 * 86400000;
rt.expiresAt = Math.min(Date.now() + 7 * 86400000, absolute);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
rt.expires_at = datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(days=7)
```
Secure:
```
absolute = rt.issued_at + timedelta(days=30)
rt.expires_at = min(datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(days=7), absolute)
```
embed_text:
Refresh tokens must have an absolute expiration, even when sliding expiration is applied, so the token cannot be renewed indefinitely.
A refresh token with sliding expiration is renewed on every use with no absolute cap, so a stolen token can be kept alive forever as long as the attacker keeps redeeming it.
The refresh token records an absolute maximum lifetime fixed at issue time; sliding renewal extends the window only up to that hard cap, after which the token is rejected and re-authentication is required.

An authorized user must be able to revoke refresh tokens and reference access tokens through the authorization server's user interface, to contain malicious clients or stolen tokens.
Once issued, tokens cannot be revoked before expiry and the user has no way to see or terminate active grants, so a stolen token stays valid until it naturally expires.
The authorization server exposes a session and token management view where the user lists active grants and revokes any refresh or reference access token, which immediately invalidates it server-side.

A confidential client must be authenticated on every client-to-server backchannel request, including token requests, pushed authorization requests, and token revocation requests.
The token, PAR, or revocation endpoint accepts a request that names a confidential client without verifying client credentials, so anyone can act as that client on the backchannel.
Every backchannel endpoint authenticates the confidential client — via client secret, private_key_jwt, or mTLS — before processing the request, and rejects any unauthenticated or mismatched credential.

**Security Rule:** The authorization server configuration must assign each OAuth client only the scopes it actually requires, so a client cannot request or receive privileges beyond its purpose.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A client is configured with the full catalogue of scopes regardless of need, so a compromised client or token can reach data and operations the application was never designed to expose to it.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each client registration lists a narrow allowed-scopes set, and the authorization server clamps any scope request to that set, never issuing a token with a scope the client is not entitled to.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client.AllowedScopes = Scopes.All;
```
Secure:
```
client.AllowedScopes = new[] { "read:profile", "read:orders" };
var granted = requested.Intersect(client.AllowedScopes);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client.allowedScopes = allScopes
```
Secure:
```
client.allowedScopes = {"read:profile", "read:orders"}
granted = requestedScopes intersect client.allowedScopes
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client.setAllowedScopes(Scopes.ALL);
```
Secure:
```
client.setAllowedScopes(Set.of("read:profile", "read:orders"));
requested.retainAll(client.getAllowedScopes());
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client.allowedScopes = ALL_SCOPES;
```
Secure:
```
client.allowedScopes = ["read:profile", "read:orders"];
const granted = requested.filter(s => client.allowedScopes.includes(s));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client.allowed_scopes = ALL_SCOPES
```
Secure:
```
client.allowed_scopes = {"read:profile", "read:orders"}
granted = set(requested_scopes) & client.allowed_scopes
```

A client is configured with the full catalogue of scopes regardless of need, so a compromised client or token can reach data and operations the application was never designed to expose to it.
Each client registration lists a narrow allowed-scopes set, and the authorization server clamps any scope request to that set, never issuing a token with a scope the client is not entitled to.

**Security Rule:** For each client the authorization server must allow only the response_mode value that client needs, validating it against the expected values or relying on PAR or JWT-secured authorization requests.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The authorization endpoint honours any response_mode the request asks for, so an attacker can switch a client to a mode such as fragment or form_post that leaks the response to an unintended sink.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each client registration pins the permitted response_mode, and the authorization server rejects any request whose response_mode is not on that per-client list.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return Respond(Request.Query["response_mode"]);
```
Secure:
```
var mode = Request.Query["response_mode"].ToString();
if (!client.AllowedResponseModes.Contains(mode))
  return BadRequest("invalid response_mode");
return Respond(mode);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
respondUsing(req.response_mode)
```
Secure:
```
if req.response_mode not in client.allowedResponseModes: reject()
respondUsing(req.response_mode)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return respond(request.getParameter("response_mode"));
```
Secure:
```
String mode = request.getParameter("response_mode");
if (!client.getAllowedResponseModes().contains(mode))
    throw new InvalidRequestException("invalid response_mode");
return respond(mode);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
respond(req.query.response_mode);
```
Secure:
```
if (!client.allowedResponseModes.includes(req.query.response_mode))
  return res.status(400).send("invalid response_mode");
respond(req.query.response_mode);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
mode = request.args.get("response_mode", "query")
return respond(mode)
```
Secure:
```
mode = request.args.get("response_mode", "query")
if mode not in client.allowed_response_modes:
    abort(400, "invalid response_mode")
return respond(mode)
```

Each client registration pins the permitted response_mode, and the authorization server rejects any request whose response_mode is not on that per-client list.

**Security Rule:** When the code grant is used, it must always be combined with pushed authorization requests so authorization parameters are sent over the backchannel rather than through the browser.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Authorization parameters travel as front-channel query string values, so they can be observed or tampered with in the browser before the authorization server ever sees them.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The client first pushes the authorization parameters to the PAR endpoint over an authenticated backchannel call and then sends the user to the authorization endpoint with only the opaque request_uri.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return Redirect($"/authorize?client_id={cid}&scope={scope}");
```
Secure:
```
var requestUri = await _par.PushAsync(cid, scope);
return Redirect($"/authorize?client_id={cid}&request_uri={requestUri}");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
redirect("/authorize?client_id=..&scope=..&redirect_uri=..")
```
Secure:
```
requestUri = postToPAR(client, params)   # backchannel
redirect("/authorize?client_id=..&request_uri=" + requestUri)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
response.sendRedirect("/authorize?client_id=" + cid + "&scope=" + scope);
```
Secure:
```
String requestUri = parClient.push(cid, scope);
response.sendRedirect("/authorize?client_id=" + cid + "&request_uri=" + requestUri);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:
```
const requestUri = await parClient.push({ clientId: cid, scope });
res.redirect(`/authorize?client_id=${cid}&request_uri=${requestUri}`);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
return redirect(f"/authorize?client_id={cid}&scope={scope}")
```
Secure:
```
request_uri = par_client.push(client_id=cid, scope=scope)
return redirect(f"/authorize?client_id={cid}&request_uri={request_uri}")
```

Authorization parameters travel as front-channel query string values, so they can be observed or tampered with in the browser before the authorization server ever sees them.
The client first pushes the authorization parameters to the PAR endpoint over an authenticated backchannel call and then sends the user to the authorization endpoint with only the opaque request_uri.

**Security Rule:** The authorization server must issue only sender-constrained access tokens, bound either to a client certificate via mutual TLS or to a DPoP key.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The authorization server issues plain bearer access tokens, so any party that captures a token can use it without proving possession of a bound key.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Every issued access token carries a cnf claim binding it to the presenter's mTLS certificate thumbprint or DPoP public key, so the token is useless without the matching private key.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var token = handler.CreateEncodedJwt(descriptor);
```
Secure:
```
descriptor.Claims["cnf"] = new { jkt = DpopThumbprint(Request.Headers["DPoP"]) };
var token = handler.CreateEncodedJwt(descriptor);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
token = issueAccessToken(claims)   # plain bearer
```
Secure:
```
claims.cnf = { jkt: dpopKeyThumbprint(req) }
token = issueAccessToken(claims)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String token = Jwts.builder().claims(claims).signWith(key).compact();
```
Secure:
```
claims.put("cnf", Map.of("jkt", dpopThumbprint(request.getHeader("DPoP"))));
String token = Jwts.builder().claims(claims).signWith(key).compact();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const token = jwt.sign(claims, key, { algorithm: "RS256" });
```
Secure:
```
claims.cnf = { jkt: dpopThumbprint(req.headers.dpop) };
const token = jwt.sign(claims, key, { algorithm: "RS256" });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
token = jwt.encode(claims, key, algorithm="RS256")
```
Secure:
```
claims["cnf"] = {"jkt": dpop_thumbprint(request.headers["DPoP"])}
token = jwt.encode(claims, key, algorithm="RS256")
```

The authorization server must issue only sender-constrained access tokens, bound either to a client certificate via mutual TLS or to a DPoP key.
The authorization server issues plain bearer access tokens, so any party that captures a token can use it without proving possession of a bound key.
Every issued access token carries a cnf claim binding it to the presenter's mTLS certificate thumbprint or DPoP public key, so the token is useless without the matching private key.

document:
**Security Rule:** For a server-side client, the authorization server must ensure the authorization_details value originates from the client backend and was not tampered with by the user, for example by requiring PAR or JAR.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The rich authorization_details parameter is read from a front-channel request, so the user can edit it in the browser to broaden the transaction the consent screen describes.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** For server-side clients the authorization_details is supplied only through PAR or a signed JAR request object, so the value is fixed by the backend and cannot be altered between the client and the server.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var details = Request.Query["authorization_details"].ToString();
```
Secure:
```
var pushed = _parStore.Get(Request.Query["request_uri"]);
var details = pushed.AuthorizationDetails;
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
details = req.query.authorization_details
```

**Security Rule:** The client must be confidential and the authorization server must require a strong, replay-resistant, public-key client authentication method such as mutual TLS or private_key_jwt.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The client authenticates with a shared client secret, so a leaked secret from logs, a config file, or a breach lets an attacker fully impersonate the client.

**Security Rule:** The OIDC client acting as relying party must mitigate ID Token replay by checking that the nonce claim in the ID Token matches the nonce it sent in the authentication request.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The client accepts an ID Token without comparing its nonce to the value it generated, so an attacker can replay an ID Token captured from another session and have it accepted.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The client generates a random nonce, stores it bound to the user-agent session, and rejects the ID Token unless the token's nonce claim equals the stored value.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var claims = handler.ValidateToken(idToken, parms, out _);
```
Secure:
```
var claims = handler.ValidateToken(idToken, parms, out _);
if (claims.FindFirstValue("nonce") != HttpContext.Session.GetString("nonce"))
    throw new SecurityException("nonce mismatch");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:

```
Secure:

login(idToken.sub)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]

```
Secure:
```

if (!Objects.equals(c.get("nonce"), session.getAttribute("nonce")))
    throw new SecurityException("nonce mismatch");
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]

```
Secure:
```

```

    abort(400, "nonce mismatch")
```

**Security Rule:** The OIDC client must uniquely identify the user from ID Token claims that cannot be reassigned within an identity provider, normally the sub claim scoped to that provider.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The client identifies users by a mutable ID Token claim such as email or name, so a changed or reused value at the provider can let one user inherit another's local account.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The client keys the local account on the provider's immutable sub claim (scoped per issuer), so identity is stable across profile changes and freed usernames cannot be hijacked.

**Security Rule:** The OIDC client must reject authorization server metadata whose issuer URL does not exactly match the issuer URL the client was pre-configured to trust, blocking impersonation through forged metadata.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The client fetches and trusts discovery metadata without checking the issuer field, so a malicious server can serve metadata that impersonates a legitimate provider.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** After loading discovery metadata the client compares its issuer value against the pre-configured expected issuer using exact string comparison and rejects the metadata on any mismatch.

**Security Rule:** The OIDC client must confirm that an ID Token is intended for it by checking that the token's aud claim equals the client's own client_id.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The client verifies only the ID Token signature and ignores the aud claim, so a token minted for a different relying party at the same provider is accepted and can be replayed.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** After signature validation the client checks the aud claim equals its own client_id and rejects any ID Token whose audience names a different relying party.

**Security Rule:** For OIDC back-channel logout the relying party must reject malformed logout tokens — verifying the type is logout+jwt, the events claim has the correct member, and no nonce claim is present — to prevent forced logout denial of service and cross-JWT confusion.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The relying party processes any signed JWT it receives at the logout endpoint, so an ID Token or other JWT can be replayed as a logout token to forcibly terminate a victim's sessions.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The logout endpoint validates the token typ header is logout+jwt, requires the back-channel logout events member, and rejects any token that carries a nonce claim before honouring the logout.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var claims = handler.ValidateToken(logoutToken, parms, out _);
EndSession(claims.FindFirstValue("sub"));
```
Secure:
```
var jwt = handler.ReadJwtToken(logoutToken);
if (jwt.Header.Typ != "logout+jwt" || jwt.Payload.ContainsKey("nonce")
    || !jwt.Payload.ContainsKey("events"))
  return BadRequest("invalid logout token");
EndSession(jwt.Subject);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
claims = verify(logoutToken); endSession(claims.sub)
```
Secure:
```
if header.typ != "logout+jwt": reject()
if "events" not in claims or "nonce" in claims: reject()
endSession(claims.sub)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Claims c = parser.parseClaimsJws(logoutToken).getBody();
endSession(c.getSubject());
```
Secure:
```
Jws<Claims> jws = parser.parseClaimsJws(logoutToken);
if (!"logout+jwt".equals(jws.getHeader().getType())
    || jws.getBody().containsKey("nonce")
    || !jws.getBody().containsKey("events"))
  throw new InvalidTokenException("invalid logout token");
endSession(jws.getBody().getSubject());
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const claims = jwt.verify(logoutToken, key);
endSession(claims.sub);
```
Secure:
```
const { header, payload } = jwt.verify(logoutToken, key, { complete: true });
if (header.typ !== "logout+jwt" || payload.nonce || !payload.events)
  return res.status(400).send("invalid logout token");
endSession(payload.sub);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
claims = jwt.decode(logout_token, key, algorithms=["RS256"])
end_session(claims["sub"])
```
Secure:
```
header = jwt.get_unverified_header(logout_token)
claims = jwt.decode(logout_token, key, algorithms=["RS256"])
if header["typ"] != "logout+jwt" or "nonce" in claims \
        or "http://schemas.openid.net/event/backchannel-logout" not in claims["events"]:
    abort(400, "invalid logout token")
end_session(claims["sub"])
```
embed_text:
For OIDC back-channel logout the relying party must reject malformed logout tokens — verifying the type is logout+jwt, the events claim has the correct member, and no nonce claim is present — to prevent forced logout denial of service and cross-JWT confusion.
The relying party processes any signed JWT it receives at the logout endpoint, so an ID Token or other JWT can be replayed as a logout token to forcibly terminate a victim's sessions.
The logout endpoint validates the token typ header is logout+jwt, requires the back-channel logout events member, and rejects any token that carries a nonce claim before honouring the logout.

The OpenID Provider must allow only the response types code, ciba, id_token, or id_token code; the token implicit flow must never be permitted and plain code is preferred over the hybrid flow.
The provider accepts response_type values including token, enabling an implicit flow that returns access tokens through the browser front channel where they leak via history and referrers.
The provider validates response_type against a fixed allowlist of code, ciba, id_token, and id_token code, and rejects any request that asks for the implicit token response.

The OpenID Provider must mitigate forced-logout denial of service by obtaining explicit end-user confirmation, or by validating logout request parameters such as the id_token_hint when present.
The provider terminates a user's session on receipt of any logout request, so an attacker can forcibly log a victim out simply by triggering the logout endpoint.
The provider either prompts the user to confirm logout or validates the id_token_hint binds the request to the current session, so an unsolicited logout request cannot end the session unilaterally.

The authorization server must obtain user consent for each authorization request, and must always explicitly prompt for consent when the client's identity cannot be assured.
The authorization server silently reuses a prior consent or skips the consent screen entirely, so a client gains authorization the user never knowingly approved.
The authorization server checks for a valid recorded consent covering the requested scopes and client, and always shows an explicit consent prompt when the client is untrusted or no matching consent exists.

**Security Rule:** When prompting for consent the authorization server must present clear and sufficient information — the nature of the requested authorizations, the identity of the application, and the lifetime of the grant.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The consent screen shows only raw scope strings or an opaque client name, so the user cannot understand what access they are granting, to whom, or for how long.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The consent screen renders human-readable descriptions of each requested scope or authorization detail, the verified application identity, and the lifetime of the authorization being granted.

```

embed_text:
When prompting for consent the authorization server must present clear and sufficient information — the nature of the requested authorizations, the identity of the application, and the lifetime of the grant.
The consent screen shows only raw scope strings or an opaque client name, so the user cannot understand what access they are granting, to whom, or for how long.
The consent screen renders human-readable descriptions of each requested scope or authorization detail, the verified application identity, and the lifetime of the authorization being granted.

The user must be able to review, modify, and revoke the consents they have granted through the authorization server.
Once consent is granted the user has no interface to see or withdraw it, so a client keeps its authorization indefinitely even after the user no longer wants to allow it.
The authorization server provides a consent management view where the user lists every granted authorization and can narrow its scopes or revoke it, with revocation invalidating the associated tokens.

**Security Rule:** Maintain a documented key-management policy and key lifecycle aligned with a recognized standard such as NIST SP 800-57, and ensure keys are not overshared — shared secrets with at most two entities and private keys held by a single entity.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Cryptographic keys are created, distributed, and retired ad hoc with no documented policy, and the same key or private key is shared widely across services, users, or environments.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A written key-management policy defines generation, rotation, escrow, and destruction for every key, and access is scoped so each key is held by the minimum number of entities its purpose allows.

```

**Security Rule:** Keep a maintained and regularly updated cryptographic inventory of all keys, algorithms, and certificates, documenting where each key may and may not be used and which data types it may and may not protect.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** There is no inventory of the algorithms, keys, and certificates the application uses, so weak ciphers, expired certificates, and misused keys go unnoticed and key purpose is never enforced.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A living inventory records every algorithm, key, and certificate with its allowed scope and data classification, and is reviewed on a schedule so deprecated or misused crypto is found and remediated.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Cryptographic usage is tracked only by memory or manual notes, so forgotten or third-party crypto calls are never discovered and cannot be audited or migrated.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Automated discovery tooling scans code, dependencies, and runtime to enumerate all encryption, hashing, and signing operations, feeding the cryptographic inventory continuously.

**Security Rule:** Maintain a cryptographic inventory that includes a documented migration plan describing the path to new cryptographic standards, such as post-quantum cryptography, so future threats can be answered.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The inventory exists but there is no migration plan, so when an algorithm is broken or post-quantum cryptography becomes mandatory there is no roadmap to move keys, data, and protocols.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The inventory is paired with a documented migration plan that maps each algorithm to its successor, sequences the cutover, and identifies the data that must be re-protected under post-quantum schemes.

```

**Security Rule:** Design the application with crypto agility so algorithms, key lengths, rounds, ciphers, and modes can be reconfigured or swapped at any time, and keys, passwords, and encrypted data can be replaced and re-encrypted.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The cipher, mode, and key length are hard-coded throughout the code, so when an algorithm is broken or a post-quantum scheme is needed the application cannot be upgraded without a rewrite.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Algorithm choices live in configuration behind an indirection layer, ciphertext is tagged with the scheme that produced it, and a re-encryption path exists so keys and data can be migrated cleanly.

**Security Rule:** Ensure every cryptographic primitive provides at least 128 bits of security given its algorithm, key size, and configuration — for example a 256-bit ECC key or a 3072-bit RSA key.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Keys are generated with sizes below the 128-bit security threshold, such as 1024-bit RSA or 80-bit symmetric keys, making the protected data feasible to recover with current resources.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Key sizes are chosen so each primitive meets or exceeds 128 bits of security — at least 3072-bit RSA, 256-bit ECC, or 256-bit AES — based on published equivalence tables.

**Security Rule:** Make all cryptographic operations constant-time, with no short-circuit behavior in comparisons, calculations, or returns, so timing differences do not leak secret information.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Secret values such as MACs, tokens, or password hashes are compared with an early-exit equality check, so the time taken reveals how many leading bytes matched and the secret can be recovered byte by byte.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Comparisons of secret material use a constant-time equality function that always examines every byte, so the operation duration is independent of the input and leaks nothing.

**Security Rule:** Ensure cryptographic modules fail securely and handle errors so they do not create new vulnerabilities such as padding oracle attacks.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Decryption code distinguishes a padding error from an authentication or other failure in its response or timing, so an attacker can use the error as an oracle to recover plaintext.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Decryption uses authenticated encryption or verifies a MAC before decrypting, and every failure path returns one indistinguishable error so no padding or structure detail is revealed.

**Security Rule:** Never reuse a nonce or initialization vector for more than one encryption key and data-element pair, and generate single-use numbers by a method appropriate for the algorithm.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A fixed or counter-reset IV or nonce is reused with the same key, so in stream or GCM modes the keystream repeats and the authentication guarantee collapses.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A fresh, unique nonce is generated for every encryption under a given key — random for GCM or a strictly monotonic counter — and it is never repeated for that key.

**Security Rule:** When an encryption algorithm is combined with a MAC, operate in encrypt-then-MAC mode — compute the MAC over the ciphertext, not the plaintext.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The MAC is computed over the plaintext and then encrypted, or computed in parallel, so the ciphertext is decrypted before its integrity is checked and padding oracle and forgery attacks become possible.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The MAC is computed over the finished ciphertext and verified in constant time before any decryption occurs, so tampered messages are rejected without ever being processed.

**Security Rule:** Generate every non-guessable random number or string with a cryptographically secure pseudo-random number generator and at least 128 bits of entropy; UUIDs do not satisfy this requirement.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Security-sensitive values such as session tokens, password-reset codes, or API keys are generated with a non-cryptographic random source or a UUID, so they are predictable or low in entropy.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Non-guessable values come from a cryptographically secure PRNG with at least 128 bits of entropy, encoded for transport, and not derived from a general-purpose random generator or UUID.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var token = new Random().Next().ToString();
```
Secure:
```
var token = Convert.ToHexString(RandomNumberGenerator.GetBytes(32));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
token = random_int()                # non-cryptographic PRNG
```
Secure:
```
token = csprng_bytes(32)             # 256 bits from a CSPRNG
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String token = Long.toString(new Random().nextLong());
```
Secure:
```
byte[] b = new byte[32];
SecureRandom.getInstanceStrong().nextBytes(b);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const token = Math.random().toString(36).slice(2);
```
Secure:
```
const token = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString("hex");
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
import random
token = str(random.random())
```
Secure:
```
import secrets
token = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
```
embed_text:
Generate every non-guessable random number or string with a cryptographically secure pseudo-random number generator and at least 128 bits of entropy; UUIDs do not satisfy this requirement.
Security-sensitive values such as session tokens, password-reset codes, or API keys are generated with a non-cryptographic random source or a UUID, so they are predictable or low in entropy.
Non-guessable values come from a cryptographically secure PRNG with at least 128 bits of entropy, encoded for transport, and not derived from a general-purpose random generator or UUID.

**Security Rule:** Ensure the random number generation mechanism is designed to keep working securely even under heavy demand, without degrading entropy or blocking unpredictably.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Under load the random source falls back to a weaker generator, repeats output, or depletes its entropy pool, so values that must be unpredictable become guessable during traffic spikes.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The CSPRNG is backed by a properly seeded, non-depleting source that sustains throughput under load, and high-rate generation is verified to never silently fall back to a weak source.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var rng = new Random();   // shared, not thread-safe under load
```
Secure:
```
var bytes = RandomNumberGenerator.GetBytes(32);  // OS-backed CSPRNG
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
rng = fast_prng_seeded_once_at_boot()   # degrades under heavy use
```
Secure:
```
rng = os_csprng()    # kernel CSPRNG, reseeds, safe under load
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
SecureRandom sr = SecureRandom.getInstance("SHA1PRNG");
```
Secure:
```
SecureRandom sr = SecureRandom.getInstanceStrong();
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
rng = random.Random(seed)   # same stream reused under concurrency
```
Secure:
```
import secrets
value = secrets.token_bytes(32)   # os.urandom-backed, non-blocking
```
embed_text:
Ensure the random number generation mechanism is designed to keep working securely even under heavy demand, without degrading entropy or blocking unpredictably.
Under load the random source falls back to a weaker generator, repeats output, or depletes its entropy pool, so values that must be unpredictable become guessable during traffic spikes.
The CSPRNG is backed by a properly seeded, non-depleting source that sustains throughput under load, and high-rate generation is verified to never silently fall back to a weak source.

**Security Rule:** Use only approved algorithms and modes for key generation, seeding, and digital signature operations, and ensure key generation never produces insecure keys vulnerable to known attacks such as Fermat factorization of RSA keys.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Keys are generated with an unapproved algorithm, a poor random seed, or parameters that yield weak keys — such as RSA primes too close together — that are vulnerable to factorization or forgery.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Key generation and signing use a vetted library with approved algorithms, a strong random seed, and parameter checks, so generated keys are not susceptible to known attacks.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
using var rsa = RSA.Create(1024);
```
Secure:
```
using var rsa = RSA.Create(3072);   // OS-vetted key generation
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
key = generate_rsa(bits=1024, seed=fixed_seed)
```
Secure:
```
key = vetted_lib.generate_rsa(bits=3072)   # CSPRNG-seeded, checked
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
KeyPairGenerator g = KeyPairGenerator.getInstance("RSA");
g.initialize(1024, new Random());
```
Secure:
```
KeyPairGenerator g = KeyPairGenerator.getInstance("RSA");
g.initialize(3072, SecureRandom.getInstanceStrong());
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
key = RSA.construct((n, e), consistency_check=False)  # unchecked params
```
Secure:
```
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric import rsa
key = rsa.generate_private_key(public_exponent=65537, key_size=3072)
```
embed_text:
Use only approved algorithms and modes for key generation, seeding, and digital signature operations, and ensure key generation never produces insecure keys vulnerable to known attacks such as Fermat factorization of RSA keys.
Keys are generated with an unapproved algorithm, a poor random seed, or parameters that yield weak keys — such as RSA primes too close together — that are vulnerable to factorization or forgery.
Key generation and signing use a vetted library with approved algorithms, a strong random seed, and parameter checks, so generated keys are not susceptible to known attacks.

**Security Rule:** Use approved cryptographic algorithms for key exchange, such as Diffie-Hellman, with secure parameters, to prevent attacks on key establishment that could enable adversary-in-the-middle or cryptographic breaks.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Key exchange uses weak or unvalidated parameters — a small DH group, an unverified peer public key, or a non-approved curve — allowing an adversary to recover the shared secret or intercept the channel.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Key exchange uses an approved algorithm with strong, validated parameters — a 2048-bit-or-larger DH group or an approved curve such as X25519 — and peer public keys are validated before use.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
using var dh = new ECDiffieHellmanCng(256) { HashAlgorithm = CngAlgorithm.MD5 };
```
Secure:
```
using var ecdh = ECDiffieHellman.Create(ECCurve.NamedCurves.nistP256);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
shared = dh_exchange(group_bits=512, peer_pub)   # tiny, unchecked group
```
Secure:
```
shared = ecdh_exchange(curve="X25519", validate_peer=True)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
KeyPairGenerator.getInstance("DH").initialize(512);
```
Secure:
```
KeyPairGenerator g = KeyPairGenerator.getInstance("XDH");
g.initialize(new NamedParameterSpec("X25519"));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
params = dh.generate_parameters(generator=2, key_size=512)
```
Secure:
```
from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric.x25519 import \
    X25519PrivateKey
shared = X25519PrivateKey.generate().exchange(peer_public_key)
```
embed_text:
Use approved cryptographic algorithms for key exchange, such as Diffie-Hellman, with secure parameters, to prevent attacks on key establishment that could enable adversary-in-the-middle or cryptographic breaks.
Key exchange uses weak or unvalidated parameters — a small DH group, an unverified peer public key, or a non-approved curve — allowing an adversary to recover the shared secret or intercept the channel.
Key exchange uses an approved algorithm with strong, validated parameters — a 2048-bit-or-larger DH group or an approved curve such as X25519 — and peer public keys are validated before use.

**Security Rule:** Use full memory encryption to protect sensitive data while it is in use, preventing access by unauthorized users or processes.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Sensitive data is held in plaintext in process memory while in use, so a memory dump, swap file, or another process on the host can read it directly.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Sensitive in-use data is protected by full memory encryption or a confidential-computing enclave, so plaintext is never exposed in readable host memory.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
secret = load_plaintext_secret()   # resides unencrypted in RAM
```
Secure:
```
with enclave.protected_memory() as mem:   # memory-encrypted region
    mem.store(load_secret())
```
embed_text:
Use full memory encryption to protect sensitive data while it is in use, preventing access by unauthorized users or processes.
Sensitive data is held in plaintext in process memory while in use, so a memory dump, swap file, or another process on the host can read it directly.
Sensitive in-use data is protected by full memory encryption or a confidential-computing enclave, so plaintext is never exposed in readable host memory.

**Security Rule:** Apply data minimization so the least amount of sensitive data is exposed during processing, and re-encrypt or discard that data immediately after use or as soon as feasible.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Decrypted sensitive data is loaded in full and left in memory long after the operation that needed it, widening the window in which it can be leaked or scraped.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Only the minimal fields needed are decrypted, they are used promptly, and the plaintext is zeroized or re-encrypted immediately afterwards to shrink the exposure window.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
string secret = Decrypt(blob);   // immutable, lingers until GC
```
Secure:
```
byte[] secret = Decrypt(blob);
try { Process(secret); }
finally { CryptographicOperations.ZeroMemory(secret); }
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
record = decrypt_entire_record(blob)   # full plaintext kept around
```
Secure:
```
field = decrypt_field(blob, "ssn")     # decrypt only what is needed
use(field); zeroize(field)             # wipe right after use
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String secret = new String(decrypt(blob));   // immutable, cannot wipe
```
Secure:
```
char[] secret = decryptToChars(blob);
try { process(secret); } finally { Arrays.fill(secret, '\0'); }
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
data = decrypt(blob)
process(data)   # 'data' lingers in memory for the rest of the request
```
Secure:
```
buf = bytearray(decrypt(blob))
try:
    process(buf)
finally:
    for i in range(len(buf)): buf[i] = 0   # zeroize after use
```
embed_text:
Apply data minimization so the least amount of sensitive data is exposed during processing, and re-encrypt or discard that data immediately after use or as soon as feasible.
Decrypted sensitive data is loaded in full and left in memory long after the operation that needed it, widening the window in which it can be leaked or scraped.
Only the minimal fields needed are decrypted, they are used promptly, and the plaintext is zeroized or re-encrypted immediately afterwards to shrink the exposure window.

**Security Rule:** Only current recommended TLS protocol versions, TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3, may be enabled, and the newest available version must be preferred for every connection.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The TLS stack still permits obsolete protocol versions such as SSL 3.0, TLS 1.0, or TLS 1.1, so a downgrade or a legacy client can negotiate a weak handshake that is vulnerable to known attacks.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The server and client are configured with a minimum protocol version of TLS 1.2, legacy versions are explicitly disabled, and TLS 1.3 is offered and preferred where supported.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ServicePointManager.SecurityProtocol = SecurityProtocolType.Tls11;
```
Secure:
```
ServicePointManager.SecurityProtocol =
    SecurityProtocolType.Tls12 | SecurityProtocolType.Tls13;
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tls.set_min_version("SSLv3")     # legacy versions still negotiable
```
Secure:
```
tls.set_min_version("TLS1.2")    # TLS 1.3 preferred, legacy disabled
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
sslEngine.setEnabledProtocols(new String[]{"SSLv3", "TLSv1"});
```
Secure:
```
sslEngine.setEnabledProtocols(new String[]{"TLSv1.3", "TLSv1.2"});
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
https.createServer({ secureProtocol: "TLSv1_method" }, app);
```
Secure:
```
https.createServer({ minVersion: "TLSv1.2" }, app);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ctx = ssl.SSLContext(ssl.PROTOCOL_TLS)   # allows TLS 1.0/1.1
```
Secure:
```
ctx = ssl.create_default_context()
ctx.minimum_version = ssl.TLSVersion.TLSv1_2
```
embed_text:
Only current recommended TLS protocol versions, TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3, may be enabled, and the newest available version must be preferred for every connection.
The TLS stack still permits obsolete protocol versions such as SSL 3.0, TLS 1.0, or TLS 1.1, so a downgrade or a legacy client can negotiate a weak handshake that is vulnerable to known attacks.
The server and client are configured with a minimum protocol version of TLS 1.2, legacy versions are explicitly disabled, and TLS 1.3 is offered and preferred where supported.

**Security Rule:** Only recommended cipher suites may be enabled with the strongest set as preferred, and L3 applications must restrict the suite list to those that provide forward secrecy.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The cipher suite list still includes weak or export-grade algorithms such as RC4, 3DES, or static-RSA key exchange, so a connection can negotiate a cipher with no forward secrecy or known cryptographic flaws.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The server advertises only strong AEAD cipher suites that use ephemeral key exchange for forward secrecy, orders them strongest first, and enforces its own preference order during negotiation.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var opts = new SslServerAuthenticationOptions();  // OS default, includes weak
```
Secure:
```
var opts = new SslServerAuthenticationOptions {
    CipherSuitesPolicy = new CipherSuitesPolicy(
        new[]{ TlsCipherSuite.TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 }) };
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tls.set_ciphers("ALL:RC4:3DES")          # weak, no forward secrecy
```
Secure:
```
tls.set_ciphers("ECDHE-ECDSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384")  # AEAD + ECDHE
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
sslEngine.setEnabledCipherSuites(new String[]{"TLS_RSA_WITH_3DES_EDE_CBC_SHA"});
```
Secure:
```
sslEngine.setEnabledCipherSuites(
    new String[]{"TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384"});
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tls.createServer({ ciphers: "ALL" }, handler);
```
Secure:
```
tls.createServer({ ciphers: "TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384:ECDHE-RSA-AES256-GCM-SHA384",
                   honorCipherOrder: true }, handler);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ctx.set_ciphers("DEFAULT:!aNULL")
```
Secure:
```
ctx.set_ciphers("ECDHE+AESGCM:ECDHE+CHACHA20")
```
embed_text:
Only recommended cipher suites may be enabled with the strongest set as preferred, and L3 applications must restrict the suite list to those that provide forward secrecy.
The cipher suite list still includes weak or export-grade algorithms such as RC4, 3DES, or static-RSA key exchange, so a connection can negotiate a cipher with no forward secrecy or known cryptographic flaws.
The server advertises only strong AEAD cipher suites that use ephemeral key exchange for forward secrecy, orders them strongest first, and enforces its own preference order during negotiation.

**Security Rule:** When mutual TLS is used, the application must verify that the client certificate chains to a trusted CA before the certificate identity is used for authentication or authorization.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The application reads the subject or SAN from a presented client certificate and trusts the identity without confirming the certificate was validated against a trusted CA, so a self-signed or forged certificate is accepted.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The TLS layer is configured to require and verify the client certificate against a trusted CA bundle, and the application uses the certificate identity only after that verification has succeeded.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var opts = new SslServerAuthenticationOptions {
    ClientCertificateRequired = true };  // no validation callback
```
Secure:
```
var opts = new SslServerAuthenticationOptions {
    ClientCertificateRequired = true,
    RemoteCertificateValidationCallback = ValidateAgainstTrustedCa };
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
identity = clientCert.subject           # used without chain verification
```
Secure:
```
if verifyChain(clientCert, trustedCAs): identity = clientCert.subject
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
sslEngine.setWantClientAuth(true);   // request only, not enforced
```
Secure:
```
sslEngine.setNeedClientAuth(true);   // chain verified against truststore
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
https.createServer({ requestCert: true }, (req, res) => {
  const user = req.socket.getPeerCertificate().subject.CN; });
```
Secure:
```
https.createServer({ requestCert: true, rejectUnauthorized: true,
  ca: [fs.readFileSync("trusted-ca.pem")] }, handler);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ctx.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_NONE
user = peer_cert["subject"]
```
Secure:
```
ctx.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_REQUIRED
ctx.load_verify_locations("trusted-ca.pem")
```
embed_text:
When mutual TLS is used, the application must verify that the client certificate chains to a trusted CA before the certificate identity is used for authentication or authorization.
The application reads the subject or SAN from a presented client certificate and trusts the identity without confirming the certificate was validated against a trusted CA, so a self-signed or forged certificate is accepted.
The TLS layer is configured to require and verify the client certificate against a trusted CA bundle, and the application uses the certificate identity only after that verification has succeeded.

**Security Rule:** Certificate revocation checking, such as OCSP stapling, must be enabled and configured so that revoked server certificates are detected and rejected.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The TLS endpoint performs no revocation checking, so a certificate that has been revoked after a key compromise is still accepted by clients as valid.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The server is configured to staple a fresh OCSP response in the handshake, and clients enforce revocation checks so a revoked certificate causes the connection to fail.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var opts = new SslClientAuthenticationOptions {
    CertificateRevocationCheckMode = X509RevocationMode.NoCheck };
```
Secure:
```
var opts = new SslClientAuthenticationOptions {
    CertificateRevocationCheckMode = X509RevocationMode.Online };
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tls.ocsp_stapling = false           # revocation never checked
```
Secure:
```
tls.ocsp_stapling = true            # stapled OCSP response served
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tls.createServer({ cert, key }, handler);   # no stapling enabled
```
Secure:
```
const server = tls.createServer({ cert, key }, handler);
server.on("OCSPRequest", (cert, issuer, cb) => cb(null, ocspResponse));
```
embed_text:
Certificate revocation checking, such as OCSP stapling, must be enabled and configured so that revoked server certificates are detected and rejected.
The TLS endpoint performs no revocation checking, so a certificate that has been revoked after a key compromise is still accepted by clients as valid.
The server is configured to staple a fresh OCSP response in the handshake, and clients enforce revocation checks so a revoked certificate causes the connection to fail.

**Security Rule:** Encrypted Client Hello must be enabled in the application's TLS settings so that sensitive handshake metadata such as the Server Name Indication is not exposed in plaintext.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The TLS handshake sends the Server Name Indication and other Client Hello fields in cleartext, so a network observer learns which service or virtual host the client is connecting to.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Encrypted Client Hello is enabled and the necessary ECH configuration is published, so the sensitive parts of the Client Hello including the SNI are encrypted to the server during the handshake.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
tls.ech_enabled = false             # SNI sent in cleartext
```
Secure:
```
tls.ech_enabled = true              # Client Hello inner encrypted
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ctx = ssl.create_default_context()  # ECH not configured
```
Secure:
```
ctx = ssl.create_default_context()
ctx.set_ech_config(published_ech_config)   # encrypt SNI in handshake
```
embed_text:
Encrypted Client Hello must be enabled in the application's TLS settings so that sensitive handshake metadata such as the Server Name Indication is not exposed in plaintext.
The TLS handshake sends the Server Name Indication and other Client Hello fields in cleartext, so a network observer learns which service or virtual host the client is connecting to.
Encrypted Client Hello is enabled and the necessary ECH configuration is published, so the sensitive parts of the Client Hello including the SNI are encrypted to the server during the handshake.

**Security Rule:** All connectivity between a client and external facing HTTP-based services must use TLS, and the connection must never fall back to plaintext or unencrypted communication.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An external facing endpoint is reachable over plain HTTP, or HTTPS requests silently downgrade to HTTP, so credentials and data travel the network in cleartext.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Services are exposed only over HTTPS, plain-HTTP requests are redirected to HTTPS and protected with HSTS, and clients require an https scheme so no plaintext fallback is possible.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var resp = await client.GetAsync("http://api.example.com/data");
```
Secure:
```
var resp = await client.GetAsync("https://api.example.com/data");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
fetch("http://api.example.com/login")    # plaintext endpoint
```
Secure:
```
fetch("https://api.example.com/login")   # TLS enforced, HSTS set
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
URL url = new URL("http://api.example.com/data");
```
Secure:
```
URL url = new URL("https://api.example.com/data");
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
app.listen(80, () => serve());
```
Secure:
```
app.use((req, res, next) => req.secure ? next()
  : res.redirect("https://" + req.headers.host + req.url));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
requests.get("http://api.example.com/data")
```
Secure:
```
requests.get("https://api.example.com/data")
```
embed_text:
All connectivity between a client and external facing HTTP-based services must use TLS, and the connection must never fall back to plaintext or unencrypted communication.
An external facing endpoint is reachable over plain HTTP, or HTTPS requests silently downgrade to HTTP, so credentials and data travel the network in cleartext.
Services are exposed only over HTTPS, plain-HTTP requests are redirected to HTTPS and protected with HSTS, and clients require an https scheme so no plaintext fallback is possible.

**Security Rule:** External facing services must present TLS certificates issued by a publicly trusted certificate authority rather than self-signed or privately issued certificates.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A public endpoint serves a self-signed or internally issued certificate, so clients either reject the connection or are trained to bypass certificate warnings, which masks real attacks.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The external endpoint is provisioned with a certificate from a publicly trusted CA, for example via ACME and Let's Encrypt, and the certificate is renewed automatically before expiry.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
server.use_cert("self-signed.pem")       # not publicly trusted
```
Secure:
```
server.use_cert(acme.obtain("example.com"))  # publicly trusted CA
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
https.createServer({ cert: selfSignedCert, key }, handler);
```
Secure:
```
https.createServer({ cert: letsEncryptCert, key: letsEncryptKey }, handler);
```
embed_text:
External facing services must present TLS certificates issued by a publicly trusted certificate authority rather than self-signed or privately issued certificates.
A public endpoint serves a self-signed or internally issued certificate, so clients either reject the connection or are trained to bypass certificate warnings, which masks real attacks.
The external endpoint is provisioned with a certificate from a publicly trusted CA, for example via ACME and Let's Encrypt, and the certificate is renewed automatically before expiry.

**Security Rule:** All inbound and outbound connections, including those to monitoring, management, SSH, middleware, databases, and partner systems, must use an encrypted protocol such as TLS with no fallback to plaintext.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A backend connection to a database, cache, message broker, or partner API is made over an unencrypted protocol, so credentials and data on that internal link can be read by anyone on the network path.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Every service connection is configured to require TLS or an equivalent encrypted transport, and the client refuses to proceed if encryption cannot be negotiated.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var conn = "Host=db;Database=app;SSL Mode=Disable";
```
Secure:
```
var conn = "Host=db;Database=app;SSL Mode=VerifyFull";
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
db.connect("postgres://user:pw@db/app?sslmode=disable")
```
Secure:
```
db.connect("postgres://user:pw@db/app?sslmode=verify-full")
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var url = "jdbc:postgresql://db/app?ssl=false";
```
Secure:
```
var url = "jdbc:postgresql://db/app?ssl=true&sslmode=verify-full";
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const client = new Client({ host: "db", ssl: false });
```
Secure:
```
const client = new Client({ host: "db",
  ssl: { rejectUnauthorized: true, ca: caCert } });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
psycopg.connect(host="db", dbname="app", sslmode="disable")
```
Secure:
```
psycopg.connect(host="db", dbname="app", sslmode="verify-full")
```
embed_text:
All inbound and outbound connections, including those to monitoring, management, SSH, middleware, databases, and partner systems, must use an encrypted protocol such as TLS with no fallback to plaintext.
A backend connection to a database, cache, message broker, or partner API is made over an unencrypted protocol, so credentials and data on that internal link can be read by anyone on the network path.
Every service connection is configured to require TLS or an equivalent encrypted transport, and the client refuses to proceed if encryption cannot be negotiated.

**Security Rule:** TLS clients must validate the certificate presented by the TLS server before exchanging any application data with it.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The TLS client disables or ignores certificate verification, for example by accepting any certificate or any hostname, so an attacker presenting any certificate can intercept the connection.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The client uses the default verifying TLS configuration that checks the certificate chain against trusted CAs and confirms the hostname matches, and never overrides that with a permissive callback.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Internal microservices call each other over plain HTTP on the assumption the network is trusted, so a foothold inside the network exposes all east-west traffic in cleartext.

**Security Rule:** TLS connections between internal services must use trusted certificates, and where internal or self-signed certificates are used the consumer must trust only specific internal CAs or specific pinned certificates.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An internal client disables verification entirely to tolerate self-signed certificates, so it will accept any certificate from any peer instead of just the intended internal ones.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The internal client keeps verification enabled but narrows its trust to a dedicated internal CA bundle or a pinned certificate, so only the intended internal services are accepted.

**Security Rule:** Services communicating internally must use strong mutual authentication such as TLS client authentication backed by PKI, with replay-resistant mechanisms verifying the identity of each endpoint.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Intra-service calls authenticate with a shared static secret or no endpoint identity at all, so any process that obtains the secret can impersonate a service and replayed requests are accepted.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each service presents and verifies a short-lived PKI-issued certificate via mutual TLS, often managed by a service mesh, so both endpoints prove identity and replayed handshakes are rejected.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The application opens outbound connections to services or to user-supplied destinations that are never enumerated anywhere, so no one can reason about the egress surface, dependencies, or SSRF risk.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A maintained inventory lists every external service the application talks to and every flow where a user controls a connection target, so the egress surface is known, reviewable, and testable.

**Security Rule:** For each service the application uses, the documentation must define the maximum number of concurrent connections and the application's behavior when that limit is reached, including any fallback or recovery mechanism, to prevent denial of service.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Connection pool sizes and exhaustion behavior are left at unknown defaults and undocumented, so a traffic spike silently drains the pool and the application stalls with no defined recovery path.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each service has a documented connection pool limit and a defined, tested response to exhaustion, such as fast failure, a fallback path, or bounded queueing, so overload degrades predictably.

**Security Rule:** The documentation must define resource-management strategies for every external system the application uses — release procedures, timeouts, failure handling, and retry limits with delays and backoff — and mandate short timeouts with disabled or strictly limited retries for synchronous HTTP calls.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Outbound calls run with no timeout and unbounded retries, and there is no documented release strategy, so a slow dependency causes cascading delays, leaked handles, and resource exhaustion.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each dependency has a documented strategy with short timeouts, bounded retries using exponential backoff, and explicit release of handles, so a failing dependency degrades quickly and in isolation.

```

```

    .connectTimeout(Duration.ofSeconds(3)).build();
```

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Security-critical secrets are used with no inventory and no rotation schedule, so a leaked or stale credential can remain valid indefinitely and no one knows which secrets matter most.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A documented secrets register names each critical secret and assigns it a rotation interval based on risk, so rotation is planned, auditable, and tied to the threat model.

**Security Rule:** Communication between backend components that fall outside the standard user session mechanism must be authenticated using individual service accounts, short-lived tokens, or certificates — never unchanging shared credentials such as static passwords or API keys on privileged shared accounts.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Backend services authenticate to each other with a long-lived static API key or a shared privileged account, so a single leaked secret grants broad access and rotation or attribution is impossible.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each backend service holds its own identity and authenticates with a short-lived token or client certificate, so credentials expire quickly and every call is attributable to one service.

**Security Rule:** When a credential must be used for service authentication, it must not be a vendor default such as root/root or admin/admin; default credentials must be changed before the service is reachable.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A service is deployed with the credentials it shipped with, so any attacker who knows the well-known default for that product can authenticate immediately.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Default credentials are replaced with unique, strong, generated secrets as part of provisioning, and deployment checks fail if a known default is still in place.

**Security Rule:** An allowlist must define the external resources or systems the application may communicate with for outbound requests, data loads, or file access; it may be enforced at the application, web server, firewall, or a combination of layers.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The application makes outbound requests to any host derived from input or configuration with no allowlist, so a crafted URL can reach internal services or cloud metadata endpoints via SSRF.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Before any outbound connection, the resolved destination is checked against an allowlist of permitted hosts, and anything not on the list is rejected.

STRIPE_KEY = vault_client.read("secret/data/stripe")["data"]["key"]
```
embed_text:
Backend secrets must be created, stored, accessed, and destroyed through a secrets management solution such as a key vault, never committed to source code or build artifacts; an L3 application must use a hardware-backed solution such as an HSM.
Secrets are hardcoded in source files or baked into build artifacts and container images, so anyone with repository or image access obtains them and rotation requires a rebuild.
Secrets are stored in a managed vault and fetched at runtime by the application using its own identity, so source and artifacts stay secret-free and access is centrally controlled and audited.

**Security Rule:** Access to secret assets must follow the principle of least privilege, so each identity can read only the specific secrets its function requires.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Every service or operator can read every secret in the vault because one broad policy grants access to all paths, so any single compromise exposes the full secret store.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Vault policies are scoped per identity to only the secret paths that identity needs, so a compromised service or account can read just its own narrow set of secrets.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
grant(service, secrets="*")          # read every secret
```
Secure:
```
grant(service="orders", secrets="secret/orders/")  # scoped path
```

Vulnerable:
```
policy = {"path": {"secret/*": {"capabilities": ["read"]}}}
```
Secure:
```
policy = {"path": {"secret/data/orders/*": {"capabilities": ["read"]}}}
```
embed_text:
Access to secret assets must follow the principle of least privilege, so each identity can read only the specific secrets its function requires.
Every service or operator can read every secret in the vault because one broad policy grants access to all paths, so any single compromise exposes the full secret store.
Vault policies are scoped per identity to only the secret paths that identity needs, so a compromised service or account can read just its own narrow set of secrets.

**Security Rule:** All cryptographic operations must be performed inside an isolated security module such as a vault or hardware security module, so key material is never exposed outside that module.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Private keys are loaded into application memory to sign or decrypt, so a memory disclosure, crash dump, or compromised process leaks the raw key material.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The application sends data to the security module and receives the signature or plaintext back; the key never leaves the module, so a process compromise cannot exfiltrate it.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var rsa = RSA.Create();
rsa.ImportFromPem(File.ReadAllText("signing.pem"));
```
Secure:
```
var sig = await keyClient.SignAsync("signing-key",
              SignatureAlgorithm.RS256, digest);  // key stays in Key Vault
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
key = load_private_key("signing.pem")
signature = sign(key, data)
```
Secure:
```
signature = hsm.sign(key_id="signing-key", data=data)  # key stays in HSM
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
PrivateKey key = loadPemKey("signing.pem");
sig.initSign(key);
```
Secure:
```
var ks = KeyStore.getInstance("PKCS11");   // key non-extractable in HSM
sig.initSign((PrivateKey) ks.getKey("signing-key", null));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
key = serialization.load_pem_private_key(open("k.pem","rb").read(), None)
sig = key.sign(data, padding.PKCS1v15(), hashes.SHA256())
```
Secure:
```
sig = kms.sign(KeyId="signing-key", Message=data,
               SigningAlgorithm="RSASSA_PKCS1_V1_5_SHA_256")["Signature"]
```
embed_text:
All cryptographic operations must be performed inside an isolated security module such as a vault or hardware security module, so key material is never exposed outside that module.
Private keys are loaded into application memory to sign or decrypt, so a memory disclosure, crash dump, or compromised process leaks the raw key material.
The application sends data to the security module and receives the signature or plaintext back; the key never leaves the module, so a process compromise cannot exfiltrate it.

**Security Rule:** Secrets must be configured to expire and be rotated on the schedule defined in the application's documentation, so no secret remains valid indefinitely.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Secrets are created without an expiry and are never rotated, so a credential leaked years ago is still accepted and the blast radius of any exposure grows without bound.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each secret is created with a defined time-to-live and rotated on the documented interval, so stale or leaked credentials stop being accepted automatically.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await secretClient.SetSecretAsync("db-password", value);
```
Secure:
```
var s = new KeyVaultSecret("db-password", value);
s.Properties.ExpiresOn = DateTimeOffset.UtcNow.AddDays(90);
await secretClient.SetSecretAsync(s);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
vault.put_secret("db/password", value)        # no TTL
```
Secure:
```
vault.put_secret("db/password", value, ttl="90d", rotate=True)
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client.write("secret/db", password=value)
```
Secure:
```
client.write("database/roles/app", default_ttl="2160h",
             max_ttl="2160h")   # rotation enforced by the vault
```
embed_text:
Secrets must be configured to expire and be rotated on the schedule defined in the application's documentation, so no secret remains valid indefinitely.
Secrets are created without an expiry and are never rotated, so a credential leaked years ago is still accepted and the blast radius of any exposure grows without bound.
Each secret is created with a defined time-to-live and rotated on the documented interval, so stale or leaked credentials stop being accepted automatically.

**Security Rule:** The application must be deployed without source control metadata such as .git or .svn folders, or in a way that those folders are inaccessible both externally and to the application itself.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The deployed artifact still contains the .git or .svn directory under the web root, so an attacker can download repository history and recover source code, secrets, and prior versions.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The build copies only the application files and excludes version control metadata, or the server blocks access to those folders, so no repository data is ever served.

**Security Rule:** Debug modes must be disabled for all components in production environments, so debugging features and verbose internal information are never exposed.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A framework or component runs in production with debug mode enabled, so error pages expose stack traces, configuration, and an interactive console an attacker can use.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Debug mode is driven by environment configuration and is forced off in production, so users see only generic error pages with no internal detail.

else app.UseExceptionHandler("/error");
```

```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:

server.error.include-stacktrace=always
```
Secure:
```

server.error.include-stacktrace=never
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:
```

```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

```

Debug mode is driven by environment configuration and is forced off in production, so users see only generic error pages with no internal detail.

**Security Rule:** Web servers must not expose directory listings to clients unless listing that directory is an explicit and intended feature.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A directory under the web root has no index file and the server falls back to auto-generating a listing, revealing file names that were never meant to be discoverable.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Directory listing is disabled at the server or framework level, so a request for a directory without an index returns a not-found or forbidden response instead of a file index.

Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:
```

```

```

Vulnerable:

```
Secure:
```
# web.xml
<init-param><param-name>listings</param-name><param-value>false</param-value></init-param>
```

A directory under the web root has no index file and the server falls back to auto-generating a listing, revealing file names that were never meant to be discoverable.
Directory listing is disabled at the server or framework level, so a request for a directory without an index returns a not-found or forbidden response instead of a file index.

**Security Rule:** The HTTP TRACE method must not be supported in production environments, since it can reflect request data and aid information leakage.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The web server answers HTTP TRACE requests, reflecting headers and cookies back to the caller and enabling cross-site tracing style attacks.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The server rejects TRACE requests with a method-not-allowed response, so the method is unavailable in production.

Vulnerable:
```
# default IIS handler list permits TRACEVerbHandler
```
Secure:
```
# web.config

```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]

```
# server responds to HTTP TRACE requests
```
Secure:
```
# server denies TRACE: returns 405 Method Not Allowed
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
# default connector — TRACE is accepted
```

```

The web server answers HTTP TRACE requests, reflecting headers and cookies back to the caller and enabling cross-site tracing style attacks.
The server rejects TRACE requests with a method-not-allowed response, so the method is unavailable in production.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Generated API documentation or monitoring and metrics endpoints are reachable by anyone in production, revealing internal routes, schemas, and operational data.
