**Security Rule:** When the application builds outbound requests, it must validate or constrain URI and header field lengths so it does not produce requests too large for the receiving component to accept.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The application concatenates unbounded user data into an outbound URI or into headers such as Authorization or Cookie, producing a request the receiver rejects and causing a self-inflicted denial of service.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Outbound URIs and request headers are length-checked or truncated against the receiver's known limits before the request is sent, so an oversized request is never emitted.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
await http.GetAsync($"{base}/find?q={query}");
```
Secure:
```
if (query.Length > 1024) throw new ArgumentException("query too long");
await http.GetAsync($"{base}/find?q={Uri.EscapeDataString(query)}");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
get(baseUrl + "?ids=" + ",".join(userIds))   # may exceed URI limit
```
Secure:
```
url = baseUrl + "?ids=" + ",".join(userIds)
if len(url) > MAX_URI_LEN: raise TooLong("split into batched requests")
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
client.get(base + "/lookup?token=" + token);
```
Secure:
```
if (token.length() > 4096) throw new IllegalArgumentException("token too long");
client.get(base + "/lookup?token=" + URLEncoder.encode(token, UTF_8));
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
fetch(`${base}/items?filter=${filter}`);
```
Secure:
```
if (filter.length > 1024) throw new Error("filter too long for upstream");
fetch(`${base}/items?` + new URLSearchParams({ filter }));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
requests.get(f"{base}/search?q={query}")
```
Secure:
```
if len(query) > 1024: raise ValueError("query too long for upstream")
requests.get(f"{base}/search", params={"q": query})
```
embed_text:
When the application builds outbound requests, it must validate or constrain URI and header field lengths so it does not produce requests too large for the receiving component to accept.
The application concatenates unbounded user data into an outbound URI or into headers such as Authorization or Cookie, producing a request the receiver rejects and causing a self-inflicted denial of service.
Outbound URIs and request headers are length-checked or truncated against the receiver's known limits before the request is sent, so an oversized request is never emitted.

document:
**Security Rule:** GraphQL and similar expressive query layers must enforce a query allowlist, depth limit, amount limit, or cost analysis so expensive nested queries cannot cause a denial of service.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The GraphQL server executes any client query with no depth, breadth, or cost bound, so a deeply nested or cyclic query forces the resolver to do unbounded work and exhausts the server.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The server caps query depth and complexity, or accepts only persisted allowlisted queries, rejecting any request whose cost exceeds the configured budget before resolution begins.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
services.AddGraphQL(b => b.AddSchema<AppSchema>());
```
Secure:
```
services.AddGraphQL(b => b.AddSchema<AppSchema>()
                          .AddValidationRule(new MaxComplexityRule(1000, maxDepth: 10)));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
result = graphql.execute(schema, clientQuery)   # no limits
```
Secure:
```
if queryDepth(clientQuery) > 10 or queryCost(clientQuery) > 1000: reject(400)
result = graphql.execute(schema, clientQuery)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
GraphQL.newGraphQL(schema).build();
```
Secure:
```
GraphQL.newGraphQL(schema)
       .instrumentation(new MaxQueryDepthInstrumentation(10))
       .build();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const server = new ApolloServer({ schema });
```
Secure:
```
const server = new ApolloServer({
  schema,
  validationRules: [depthLimit(10), costLimit({ maxCost: 1000 })],
});
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
schema.execute(query)
```
Secure:
```
from graphql import validate
from graphql.validation import depth_limit_validator
errors = validate(schema.graphql_schema, parse(query), [depth_limit_validator(10)])
if errors: raise GraphQLError("query too deep")
```
embed_text:
GraphQL and similar expressive query layers must enforce a query allowlist, depth limit, amount limit, or cost analysis so expensive nested queries cannot cause a denial of service.
The GraphQL server executes any client query with no depth, breadth, or cost bound, so a deeply nested or cyclic query forces the resolver to do unbounded work and exhausts the server.
The server caps query depth and complexity, or accepts only persisted allowlisted queries, rejecting any request whose cost exceeds the configured budget before resolution begins.

document:
**Security Rule:** GraphQL introspection must be disabled in production unless the API is deliberately offered to external parties, so the full schema is not exposed to attackers.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Introspection is left enabled on a production GraphQL endpoint that is not a public API, so anyone can download the complete schema and map every type, field, and mutation for attack.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Introspection is disabled in production environments by configuration or a validation rule, and enabled only for internal use or for APIs explicitly intended to be consumed by third parties.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
services.AddGraphQL(b => b.AddSchema<AppSchema>());
```
Secure:
```
services.AddGraphQL(b => b.AddSchema<AppSchema>()
                          .AddSchemaVisitor(new IntrospectionDisabledIn(env)));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
server = graphqlServer(schema, introspection=true)
```
Secure:
```
server = graphqlServer(schema, introspection=(env == "development"))
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
GraphQL.newGraphQL(schema).build();
```
Secure:
```
GraphQL.newGraphQL(schema)
       .instrumentation(new NoIntrospectionGraphqlFieldVisibility())
       .build();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const server = new ApolloServer({ schema, introspection: true });
```
Secure:
```
const server = new ApolloServer({ schema, introspection: process.env.NODE_ENV !== "production" });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
GraphQLView.as_view("graphql", schema=schema)
```
Secure:
```
from graphql.validation import NoSchemaIntrospectionCustomRule
rules = [] if DEBUG else [NoSchemaIntrospectionCustomRule]
GraphQLView.as_view("graphql", schema=schema, validation_rules=rules)
```
embed_text:
GraphQL introspection must be disabled in production unless the API is deliberately offered to external parties, so the full schema is not exposed to attackers.
Introspection is left enabled on a production GraphQL endpoint that is not a public API, so anyone can download the complete schema and map every type, field, and mutation for attack.
Introspection is disabled in production environments by configuration or a validation rule, and enabled only for internal use or for APIs explicitly intended to be consumed by third parties.

document:
**Security Rule:** All WebSocket connections must use WebSocket over TLS (the wss scheme), so the channel is encrypted end to end like HTTPS traffic.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A WebSocket connection is opened with the plaintext ws scheme, so every frame travels unencrypted and any network observer can read or tamper with the session.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Connections are established exclusively with the wss scheme over TLS, and plain ws upgrade requests are refused by the server.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:

```

```

embed_text:
All WebSocket connections must use WebSocket over TLS (the wss scheme), so the channel is encrypted end to end like HTTPS traffic.
A WebSocket connection is opened with the plaintext ws scheme, so every frame travels unencrypted and any network observer can read or tamper with the session.
Connections are established exclusively with the wss scheme over TLS, and plain ws upgrade requests are refused by the server.

**Security Rule:** During the WebSocket handshake the server must check the Origin header against an allowlist of permitted origins and reject any connection from an origin not on the list.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The WebSocket server completes the handshake without inspecting the Origin header, so a script on any malicious site can open an authenticated cross-site WebSocket connection (Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking).

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The handshake handler reads the Origin header, compares it against a configured allowlist of trusted origins, and aborts the upgrade when the origin does not match.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var socket = await context.WebSockets.AcceptWebSocketAsync();
```
Secure:
```
if (!AllowedOrigins.Contains(context.Request.Headers.Origin.ToString()))
{ context.Response.StatusCode = 403; return; }
var socket = await context.WebSockets.AcceptWebSocketAsync();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
onHandshake(req): accept()          # Origin never checked
```
Secure:
```
onHandshake(req):
    if req.header("Origin") not in ALLOWED_ORIGINS: reject(403)
    accept()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
public void afterConnectionEstablished(WebSocketSession s) { }
```
Secure:
```
protected boolean checkOrigin(ServerHttpRequest req) {
    return ALLOWED_ORIGINS.contains(req.getHeaders().getOrigin());
}
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const wss = new WebSocketServer({ server });
```
Secure:
```
const wss = new WebSocketServer({ server, verifyClient: (info, done) =>
  done(ALLOWED_ORIGINS.has(info.origin)) });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
async def ws_endpoint(ws): await ws.accept()
```
Secure:
```
async def ws_endpoint(ws):
    if ws.headers.get("origin") not in ALLOWED_ORIGINS:
        await ws.close(code=1008); return
    await ws.accept()
```
embed_text:
During the WebSocket handshake the server must check the Origin header against an allowlist of permitted origins and reject any connection from an origin not on the list.
The WebSocket server completes the handshake without inspecting the Origin header, so a script on any malicious site can open an authenticated cross-site WebSocket connection (Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking).
The handshake handler reads the Origin header, compares it against a configured allowlist of trusted origins, and aborts the upgrade when the origin does not match.

**Security Rule:** When the application's standard session management cannot be used over WebSocket, the dedicated tokens used instead must satisfy the same session management security requirements.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A WebSocket channel uses an ad hoc token that is long-lived, not bound to a session, lacks entropy, or is never revoked, so it fails the protections that normal session cookies provide.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Dedicated WebSocket tokens are generated with strong entropy, scoped and time-limited, validated on every connection, and revocable, so they meet the same bar as the standard session mechanism.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var token = user.Id.ToString();
```
Secure:
```
var token = Convert.ToBase64String(RandomNumberGenerator.GetBytes(32));
await wsTokenStore.SetAsync(token, user.Id, TimeSpan.FromMinutes(5));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
wsToken = userId + ":" + appName          # guessable, never expires
```
Secure:
```
wsToken = randomSecure(32); store(wsToken, userId, ttl=300, revocable=true)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String token = Integer.toString(user.getId());
```
Secure:
```
byte[] b = new byte[32]; new SecureRandom().nextBytes(b);
String token = Base64.getUrlEncoder().encodeToString(b);
wsTokenStore.put(token, user.getId(), Duration.ofMinutes(5));
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const token = String(user.id);
```
Secure:
```
const token = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString("base64url");
await redis.set(`ws:${token}`, user.id, "EX", 300);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
token = base64(f"{user.id}".encode())
```
Secure:
```
token = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
await ws_token_store.set(token, user.id, ex=300)
```

When the application's standard session management cannot be used over WebSocket, the dedicated tokens used instead must satisfy the same session management security requirements.
A WebSocket channel uses an ad hoc token that is long-lived, not bound to a session, lacks entropy, or is never revoked, so it fails the protections that normal session cookies provide.
Dedicated WebSocket tokens are generated with strong entropy, scoped and time-limited, validated on every connection, and revocable, so they meet the same bar as the standard session mechanism.

**Security Rule:** When an existing HTTPS session transitions to a WebSocket channel, the dedicated WebSocket token must be issued or validated through that previously authenticated HTTPS session.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A WebSocket token is handed out or accepted without tying it to the user's already authenticated HTTPS session, so an unauthenticated or different client can obtain and use a channel token.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The WebSocket token is minted on an authenticated HTTPS endpoint and bound to that session, and the WebSocket handshake validates the token against the originating authenticated session before accepting.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
[HttpGet("ws-token")]
public Token WsToken() => new(Mint());
```
Secure:
```
[Authorize]
[HttpGet("ws-token")]
public Token WsToken() => _wsTokens.IssueFor(User.GetId(), TimeSpan.FromMinutes(1));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
GET /ws-token -> return newToken()        # no auth on the issuing route
```
Secure:
```
GET /ws-token (requires authenticated HTTPS session) ->
    token bound to session.user; handshake verifies that binding
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
@GetMapping("/ws-token")
public Token wsToken() { return new Token(mint()); }
```
Secure:
```
@GetMapping("/ws-token")
public Token wsToken(@AuthenticationPrincipal User user) {
    return wsTokens.issueFor(user.getId(), Duration.ofMinutes(1));
}
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
app.get("/ws-token", (req, res) => res.json({ token: newToken() }));
```
Secure:
```
app.get("/ws-token", requireAuth, (req, res) => {
  const t = mintToken(); store(t, req.user.id, 60); res.json({ token: t });
});
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
@app.get("/ws-token")
def ws_token(): return {"token": new_token()}
```
Secure:
```
@app.get("/ws-token")
def ws_token(user: User = Depends(current_active_user)):
    t = secrets.token_urlsafe(32); store(t, user.id, ttl=60); return {"token": t}
```
embed_text:
When an existing HTTPS session transitions to a WebSocket channel, the dedicated WebSocket token must be issued or validated through that previously authenticated HTTPS session.
A WebSocket token is handed out or accepted without tying it to the user's already authenticated HTTPS session, so an unauthenticated or different client can obtain and use a channel token.
The WebSocket token is minted on an authenticated HTTPS endpoint and bound to that session, and the WebSocket handshake validates the token against the originating authenticated session before accepting.

**Security Rule:** Documentation must define, for every upload feature, the permitted file types, expected extensions, and maximum size including unpacked size, and must describe how files are made safe to download and how the application behaves when a malicious file is detected.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Upload features have no documented contract for allowed types, sizes, or unpacked limits, so each implementation invents its own ad hoc rules and there is no agreed behaviour when a malicious file is found.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A written specification enumerates per-feature allowed types, extensions, and maximum raw and unpacked sizes, and defines the detection and safe-handling behaviour that every upload path follows.

Upload features have no documented contract for allowed types, sizes, or unpacked limits, so each implementation invents its own ad hoc rules and there is no agreed behaviour when a malicious file is found.
A written specification enumerates per-feature allowed types, extensions, and maximum raw and unpacked sizes, and defines the detection and safe-handling behaviour that every upload path follows.

**Security Rule:** The application must only accept uploaded files of a size it can process without loss of performance, rejecting oversized uploads before they are buffered or processed to prevent denial of service.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Uploaded files are read into memory or written to disk without any size limit, so a single very large upload exhausts memory, disk, or request-processing capacity.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A maximum upload size is enforced at the framework or server layer and the request is rejected as soon as the limit is exceeded, before the body is fully buffered.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var bytes = await new StreamReader(Request.Body).ReadToEndAsync();
```
Secure:
```
[RequestSizeLimit(10_485_760)]
public IActionResult Upload(IFormFile file) { /* ... */ }
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
data = readEntireBody(request)        # no size limit
```
Secure:
```
if contentLength(request) > MAX_UPLOAD_BYTES: reject(413)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Part part = request.getPart("file");  // no maxFileSize configured
```
Secure:
```
@MultipartConfig(maxFileSize = 10_485_760, maxRequestSize = 10_485_760)
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
app.use(express.raw({ limit: Infinity }));
```
Secure:
```
app.use(multer({ limits: { fileSize: 10 * 1024 * 1024 } }));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
content = await file.read()           # reads any size into memory
```
Secure:
```
app.config["MAX_CONTENT_LENGTH"] = 10 * 1024 * 1024  # Flask rejects oversize
```

The application must only accept uploaded files of a size it can process without loss of performance, rejecting oversized uploads before they are buffered or processed to prevent denial of service.
Uploaded files are read into memory or written to disk without any size limit, so a single very large upload exhausts memory, disk, or request-processing capacity.
A maximum upload size is enforced at the framework or server layer and the request is rejected as soon as the limit is exceeded, before the body is fully buffered.

**Security Rule:** When the application accepts a file, on its own or inside an archive, it must verify that the extension matches an expected extension and that the file contents correspond to that type, for example by checking magic bytes, re-writing images, or using a validation library.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A file is trusted on the basis of its name, extension, or client-supplied content type alone, so a malicious payload renamed to a permitted extension is accepted and later executed or served.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The extension is checked against an allowlist and the actual byte content is validated to match the declared type, by inspecting magic bytes or by parsing and re-encoding the file with a type-specific library.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (Path.GetExtension(file.FileName) == ".png") Save(file);
```
Secure:
```
var sig = reader.ReadBytes(8);
if (sig.Take(8).SequenceEqual(PngMagic)) Save(file);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if endsWith(filename, ".png"): save(file)   # trusts the name only
```
Secure:
```
if extAllowed(filename) and sniffType(bytes) == "image/png": save(file)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (filename.endsWith(".pdf")) Files.write(dest, bytes);
```
Secure:
```
String detected = new Tika().detect(bytes);
if ("application/pdf".equals(detected)) Files.write(dest, bytes);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (file.mimetype === "image/png") save(file);   // client-set value
```
Secure:
```
const { ext, mime } = await fileTypeFromBuffer(buf);
if (mime === "image/png" && ext === "png") save(buf);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if filename.endswith(".png"):
    save(upload)
```
Secure:
```
from PIL import Image
img = Image.open(upload); img.verify()      # rejects non-image bytes
```

A file is trusted on the basis of its name, extension, or client-supplied content type alone, so a malicious payload renamed to a permitted extension is accepted and later executed or served.
The extension is checked against an allowlist and the actual byte content is validated to match the declared type, by inspecting magic bytes or by parsing and re-encoding the file with a type-specific library.

**Security Rule:** Before decompressing a compressed file such as a zip, gz, docx, or odt, the application must check it against a maximum allowed uncompressed size and a maximum number of contained files.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An archive is decompressed without checking its uncompressed size or entry count, so a small highly compressed archive expands into a decompression bomb that exhausts memory or disk.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each archive entry's declared uncompressed size is summed and the entry count is tallied against fixed limits before extraction, and extraction is aborted as soon as either limit is exceeded.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ZipFile.ExtractToDirectory(zipPath, destDir);
```
Secure:
```
var entries = archive.Entries;
if (entries.Sum(e => e.Length) > MAX || entries.Count > MAX_FILES)
    throw new InvalidOperationException("zip bomb");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
for entry in archive: extract(entry)        # no size or count cap
```
Secure:
```
if totalUncompressed(archive) > MAX or count(archive) > MAX_FILES: reject()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
try (ZipInputStream z = new ZipInputStream(in)) {
    while ((e = z.getNextEntry()) != null) extract(e); }
```
Secure:
```
long total = 0; int n = 0;
for (ZipEntry e : entries) { total += e.getSize(); n++;
  if (total > MAX || n > MAX_FILES) throw new IOException("zip bomb"); }
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
fs.createReadStream(path).pipe(unzipper.Extract({ path: dest }));
```
Secure:
```
let total = 0;
for (const e of entries) { total += e.uncompressedSize;
  if (total > MAX || entries.length > MAX_FILES) throw new Error("bomb"); }
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
    z.extractall(dest)
```
Secure:
```
with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
    total = sum(i.file_size for i in z.infolist())
    if total > MAX_BYTES or len(z.infolist()) > MAX_FILES: raise ValueError
```
embed_text:
Before decompressing a compressed file such as a zip, gz, docx, or odt, the application must check it against a maximum allowed uncompressed size and a maximum number of contained files.
An archive is decompressed without checking its uncompressed size or entry count, so a small highly compressed archive expands into a decompression bomb that exhausts memory or disk.
Each archive entry's declared uncompressed size is summed and the entry count is tallied against fixed limits before extraction, and extraction is aborted as soon as either limit is exceeded.

**Security Rule:** A per-user storage quota and a maximum number of files per user must be enforced so that one user cannot fill available storage with too many files or excessively large files.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Uploads accumulate without any per-user accounting, so one account can store unlimited files and consume all shared storage, denying service to other users.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The application tracks each user's current file count and total bytes, and rejects a new upload when accepting it would exceed the configured per-user file-count or storage-byte quota.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
_db.Files.Add(new StoredFile(userId, bytes));
```
Secure:
```
var used = _db.Files.Where(f => f.UserId == userId).Sum(f => f.Size);
if (used + bytes.Length > QuotaBytes) throw new QuotaExceededException();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
store(userId, file)                         # no quota accounting
```
Secure:
```
if usedBytes(userId) + size(file) > QUOTA or fileCount(userId) >= MAX:
    reject(); else store(userId, file)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
fileRepo.save(new StoredFile(userId, bytes));
```
Secure:
```
long used = fileRepo.totalBytesForUser(userId);
if (used + bytes.length > QUOTA_BYTES) throw new QuotaExceededException();
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
save_file(user.id, upload)
```
Secure:
```
used = db.scalar(select(func.sum(File.size)).where(File.user_id == user.id))
if (used or 0) + upload.size > QUOTA_BYTES:
    raise QuotaExceeded
```
embed_text:
A per-user storage quota and a maximum number of files per user must be enforced so that one user cannot fill available storage with too many files or excessively large files.
Uploads accumulate without any per-user accounting, so one account can store unlimited files and consume all shared storage, denying service to other users.
The application tracks each user's current file count and total bytes, and rejects a new upload when accepting it would exceed the configured per-user file-count or storage-byte quota.

**Security Rule:** The application must not allow uploaded compressed files to contain symbolic links unless specifically required, and where required it must enforce an allowlist of permitted symlink targets.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An archive is extracted without inspecting entries for symlinks, so a symlink entry can point extraction or later reads at sensitive files outside the intended directory.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each archive entry is inspected and symlink entries are rejected during extraction, or where symlinks are needed their targets are checked against an allowlist before the link is created.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
ZipFile.ExtractToDirectory(zipPath, destDir);
```
Secure:
```
foreach (var e in archive.Entries)
    if ((e.ExternalAttributes >> 16 & 0xF000) == 0xA000)
        throw new InvalidOperationException("symlink in archive");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
for entry in archive: extract(entry)        # symlink entries honoured
```
Secure:
```
for entry in archive:
    if isSymlink(entry): reject(); else extract(entry)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
for (ZipEntry e : entries) extract(e);
```
Secure:
```
for (ZipEntry e : entries) {
    if ((e.getUnixMode() & 0xF000) == 0xA000) throw new IOException("symlink");
    extract(e); }
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
with tarfile.open(path) as t:
    t.extractall(dest)
```
Secure:
```
with tarfile.open(path) as t:
    for m in t.getmembers():
        if m.issym() or m.islnk(): raise ValueError("symlink in archive")
    t.extractall(dest)
```
embed_text:
The application must not allow uploaded compressed files to contain symbolic links unless specifically required, and where required it must enforce an allowlist of permitted symlink targets.
An archive is extracted without inspecting entries for symlinks, so a symlink entry can point extraction or later reads at sensitive files outside the intended directory.
Each archive entry is inspected and symlink entries are rejected during extraction, or where symlinks are needed their targets are checked against an allowlist before the link is created.

**Security Rule:** The application must reject uploaded images whose pixel dimensions exceed a configured maximum, to prevent pixel flood attacks that consume excessive memory when the image is decoded.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An uploaded image is fully decoded into a pixel buffer before its dimensions are checked, so an image declaring enormous width and height exhausts memory during decoding.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The image header is read to obtain its width and height, the pixel count is compared against a maximum, and the image is rejected before it is fully decoded into a raster buffer.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
using var img = Image.Load(stream);
```
Secure:
```
var info = Image.Identify(stream);
if ((long) info.Width * info.Height > MaxPixels) throw new InvalidDataException();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
img = decodeImage(bytes)                    # decodes before any check
```
Secure:
```
w, h = readImageDimensions(bytes)
if w * h > MAX_PIXELS: reject()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
BufferedImage img = ImageIO.read(input);
```
Secure:
```
ImageReader r = ImageIO.getImageReaders(input).next();
r.setInput(input);
if ((long) r.getWidth(0) * r.getHeight(0) > MAX_PIXELS) throw new IOException();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const img = await sharp(buf).toBuffer();
```
Secure:
```
const meta = await sharp(buf).metadata();
if (meta.width * meta.height > MAX_PIXELS) throw new Error("pixel flood");
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
img = Image.open(upload); img.load()
```
Secure:
```
Image.MAX_IMAGE_PIXELS = 25_000_000         # raises DecompressionBombError
img = Image.open(upload); img.load()
```

An uploaded image is fully decoded into a pixel buffer before its dimensions are checked, so an image declaring enormous width and height exhausts memory during decoding.
The image header is read to obtain its width and height, the pixel count is compared against a maximum, and the image is rejected before it is fully decoded into a raster buffer.

**Security Rule:** Files uploaded or generated from untrusted input and stored in a publicly served folder must not be executed as server-side program code when requested directly over HTTP.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Uploaded files are stored inside a web root where the server maps extensions to handlers, so an uploaded script with an executable extension runs as server-side code when fetched.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Uploads are stored outside the web root or in a location with script execution disabled, served only through a controller that streams bytes with a safe content type and never hands them to an interpreter.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
file.SaveAs(Server.MapPath("~/uploads/" + file.FileName));
```
Secure:
```
var path = Path.Combine("/srv/uploads", Guid.NewGuid().ToString());
await file.CopyToAsync(File.Create(path));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
save(file, "/var/www/html/uploads/" + filename)   # under the web root
```
Secure:
```
save(file, "/srv/data/uploads/" + generatedId)     # outside the web root
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Files.write(Path.of(servletContext.getRealPath("/uploads"), name), bytes);
```
Secure:
```
Files.write(Path.of("/srv/uploads", UUID.randomUUID().toString()), bytes);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
upload.save(os.path.join("static/uploads", upload.filename))
```
Secure:
```
upload.save(os.path.join("/srv/uploads", uuid4().hex))
# served via send_file with an explicit, non-executable mimetype
```

Files uploaded or generated from untrusted input and stored in a publicly served folder must not be executed as server-side program code when requested directly over HTTP.
Uploaded files are stored inside a web root where the server maps extensions to handlers, so an uploaded script with an executable extension runs as server-side code when fetched.
Uploads are stored outside the web root or in a location with script execution disabled, served only through a controller that streams bytes with a safe content type and never hands them to an interpreter.

document:
**Security Rule:** File paths for file operations must be built from internally generated or trusted data rather than user-submitted filenames, and where user filenames or metadata must be used they must be strictly validated and sanitized to prevent path traversal, LFI, RFI, and SSRF.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A user-supplied filename or path is concatenated into a file path, so traversal sequences or absolute paths let an attacker read or write files outside the intended directory or reach remote resources.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The application generates its own identifier for storage, or strips directory components and resolves the final path, confirming it stays within the intended base directory before any file operation.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var path = Path.Combine(baseDir, Request.Query["name"]);
```
Secure:
```
var full = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(baseDir, name));
if (!full.StartsWith(baseDir, StringComparison.Ordinal)) throw new();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
open(baseDir + "/" + userFilename)          # ../ escapes baseDir
```
Secure:
```
path = resolve(baseDir, basename(userFilename))
if not path.startsWith(baseDir): reject()
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
File f = new File(baseDir, request.getParameter("name"));
```
Secure:
```
Path p = Path.of(baseDir).resolve(name).normalize();
if (!p.startsWith(Path.of(baseDir))) throw new SecurityException();
```

```

```

A user-supplied filename or path is concatenated into a file path, so traversal sequences or absolute paths let an attacker read or write files outside the intended directory or reach remote resources.
The application generates its own identifier for storage, or strips directory components and resolves the final path, confirming it stays within the intended base directory before any file operation.

**Security Rule:** Server-side file processing such as archive decompression must ignore any path information embedded in the input so that extraction cannot write files outside the target directory, preventing zip slip.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Archive entry names are joined onto the destination directory as-is during extraction, so an entry name containing traversal sequences writes a file outside the intended directory — a zip slip.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each entry's name is reduced to a safe basename or the resolved destination path is verified to remain inside the target directory before any byte is written during extraction.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
entry.ExtractToFile(Path.Combine(destDir, entry.FullName));
```
Secure:
```
var target = Path.GetFullPath(Path.Combine(destDir, entry.FullName));
if (!target.StartsWith(destDir, StringComparison.Ordinal)) throw new();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
write(destDir + "/" + entry.name, entry.bytes)   # name may be ../../etc
```
Secure:
```
target = resolve(destDir, entry.name)
if not target.startsWith(destDir): reject(); else write(target, entry.bytes)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
File out = new File(destDir, entry.getName());
Files.copy(zis, out.toPath());
```
Secure:
```
Path target = Path.of(destDir).resolve(entry.getName()).normalize();
if (!target.startsWith(Path.of(destDir))) throw new IOException("zip slip");
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
fs.writeFileSync(path.join(dest, entry.fileName), entry.getData());
```
Secure:
```
const target = path.resolve(dest, entry.fileName);
if (!target.startsWith(dest + path.sep)) throw new Error("zip slip");
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
for info in z.infolist():
    open(os.path.join(dest, info.filename), "wb").write(z.read(info))
```
Secure:
```
for info in z.infolist():
    target = (Path(dest) / info.filename).resolve()
    if not target.is_relative_to(Path(dest).resolve()): raise ValueError
```

Server-side file processing such as archive decompression must ignore any path information embedded in the input so that extraction cannot write files outside the target directory, preventing zip slip.
Archive entry names are joined onto the destination directory as-is during extraction, so an entry name containing traversal sequences writes a file outside the intended directory — a zip slip.
Each entry's name is reduced to a safe basename or the resolved destination path is verified to remain inside the target directory before any byte is written during extraction.

**Security Rule:** The application must validate or ignore user-submitted filenames, including those in JSON, JSONP, or URL parameters, and must specify a filename in the Content-Disposition response header field.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A user-supplied filename is used directly to locate the file to serve or is echoed without a Content-Disposition header, allowing path traversal or letting the browser guess an unsafe handling for the file.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The download is keyed by a trusted internal identifier, the served filename is validated against an allowlist or sanitized, and a Content-Disposition header sets an explicit attachment filename.

return PhysicalFile(Path.Combine(Dir, Request.Query["file"]), "application/octet-stream");
```

var rec = _db.Files.Find(id);
return File(rec.Bytes, "application/octet-stream", rec.SafeName);
```

**Security Rule:** Filenames served in HTTP response header fields or email attachments must be encoded or sanitized, for example following RFC 6266, so they preserve document structure and cannot inject into the response.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An untrusted filename is placed unencoded into a Content-Disposition header or attachment name, so quotes, newlines, or non-ASCII bytes break the header structure or enable response splitting.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The filename is sanitized of control characters and encoded per RFC 6266, using the quoted ASCII fallback together with the filename star UTF-8 form, so it always parses as a single safe header value.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Response.Headers["Content-Disposition"] = "attachment; filename=" + name;
```
Secure:
```
var cd = new ContentDispositionHeaderValue("attachment");
cd.SetHttpFileName(name);   // emits RFC 6266 filename and filename*
Response.Headers["Content-Disposition"] = cd.ToString();
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
header("Content-Disposition: attachment; filename=" + userName)
```
Secure:
```
header("Content-Disposition: attachment; filename=\""
       + asciiFallback(userName) + "\"; filename*=UTF-8''" + rfc5987(userName))
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
response.setHeader("Content-Disposition", "attachment; filename=" + name);
```
Secure:
```
ContentDisposition cd = ContentDisposition.attachment()
    .filename(name, StandardCharsets.UTF_8).build();
response.setHeader("Content-Disposition", cd.toString());
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
res.setHeader("Content-Disposition", `attachment; filename=${name}`);
```
Secure:
```
const safe = name.replace(/[\r\n"]/g, "");
res.setHeader("Content-Disposition",
    `attachment; filename="${safe}"; filename*=UTF-8''${encodeURIComponent(name)}`);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
resp.headers["Content-Disposition"] = f'attachment; filename={name}'
```
Secure:
```
from werkzeug.utils import secure_filename
resp.headers["Content-Disposition"] = \
    f'attachment; filename="{secure_filename(name)}"'
```
embed_text:
Filenames served in HTTP response header fields or email attachments must be encoded or sanitized, for example following RFC 6266, so they preserve document structure and cannot inject into the response.
An untrusted filename is placed unencoded into a Content-Disposition header or attachment name, so quotes, newlines, or non-ASCII bytes break the header structure or enable response splitting.
The filename is sanitized of control characters and encoded per RFC 6266, using the quoted ASCII fallback together with the filename star UTF-8 form, so it always parses as a single safe header value.

**Security Rule:** Files obtained from untrusted sources must be scanned by antivirus or malware scanners before they are stored or served, to prevent the application from distributing known malicious content.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Untrusted uploads are stored and later served to other users without any malware scan, so the application becomes a distribution channel for known malicious files.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each untrusted file is submitted to an antivirus or malware scanner after upload, quarantined or rejected on a positive result, and only released for download once the scan reports it clean.

Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:
```
var result = await _antivirus.ScanAsync(file.OpenReadStream());
if (!result.IsClean) throw new MalwareDetectedException();
_storage.Save(file);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

```
Secure:
```
if scanForMalware(upload) == "clean": store(upload)
else: quarantine(upload)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
fileService.store(bytes);
```
Secure:
```
ScanResult r = antivirus.scan(new ByteArrayInputStream(bytes));
if (!r.isClean()) throw new MalwareDetectedException();
fileService.store(bytes);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
save_file(upload)
```
Secure:
```
result = clamd.ClamdUnixSocket().instream(upload.stream)
if result["stream"][0] != "OK": raise MalwareDetected
save_file(upload)
```

**Security Rule:** Application documentation must define how rate limiting, anti-automation, and adaptive response defend against credential stuffing and brute force, including how they are configured and how malicious account lockout is prevented.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Anti-automation behaviour is undocumented or ad hoc, so brute-force defences are inconsistent and an attacker can lock out legitimate users by deliberately failing logins against their accounts.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Documentation describes the rate-limiting and adaptive-response controls, their thresholds, and how they throttle attackers without letting an attacker weaponize lockout against a victim account.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
# login throttling exists in code but is undocumented and unbounded
if failures(account) > 5: lock(account)   # attacker locks any victim
```
Secure:
```
# documented: throttle by IP+account, slow down rather than hard-lock
if failures(ip, account) > 5: delay(backoff(failures))
```

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** No documented list of context-specific words exists, so users can pick passwords trivially guessable for this organization, such as the company or product name with a year appended.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** A maintained denylist of context-specific words and their permutations is documented and referenced by the password policy so these terms are rejected at registration and password change.

```

**Security Rule:** When an application offers multiple authentication pathways, all of them must be documented along with the security controls and authentication strength that must be enforced consistently across every pathway.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Alternate login pathways such as legacy APIs, mobile endpoints, or SSO fallbacks are undocumented, so each enforces different controls and an attacker uses the weakest one to bypass MFA or rate limiting.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Every authentication pathway is enumerated in documentation with the required strength and controls, so reviewers can confirm MFA, throttling, and lockout are applied uniformly.

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:

```
Secure:
```

authenticate(user, credentials, pathway)   # MFA enforced everywhere
```

**Security Rule:** User-chosen passwords must be at least 8 characters long, with a minimum of 15 characters strongly recommended.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The application accepts very short passwords, or enforces no minimum length at all, leaving accounts vulnerable to brute force and guessing.

**Security Rule:** Authenticated users must be able to change their own password through the application.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The application provides no self-service password change, so a user who suspects their password is compromised cannot rotate it without help.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** An authenticated password change endpoint is exposed so users can update their own credentials at any time.

Vulnerable:
```
[HttpPost("account/email")] public IActionResult ChangeEmail() => Ok();
```
Secure:
```

```

```
Secure:

```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]

```
Secure:

```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]

```
Secure:

```
embed_text:
Authenticated users must be able to change their own password through the application.
The application provides no self-service password change, so a user who suspects their password is compromised cannot rotate it without help.
An authenticated password change endpoint is exposed so users can update their own credentials at any time.

The password change function must require both the user's current password and the new password before the change is applied.
Password change accepts only a new password, so anyone with a hijacked session or an open unattended browser can silently take over the account.
The current password is re-verified against the stored hash before the new password is accepted, proving the requester knows the existing credential.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** No common-password check is performed, so users register with widely known passwords that appear at the top of every credential-stuffing list.

**Security Rule:** Passwords of any character composition must be accepted; no rule may require a minimum count of uppercase, lowercase, digit, or special characters.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Composition rules force a mix of character classes, which pushes users toward predictable patterns and rejects strong passphrases that lack a digit or symbol.

**Security Rule:** A password must remain valid until it is found to be compromised or the user chooses to rotate it; routine periodic expiry must not be required.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Passwords are force-expired on a fixed schedule, which trains users to pick incremental variants and rotate to weaker, predictable passwords.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Passwords have no scheduled expiry; rotation is triggered only by a detected compromise or an explicit user request.

```

```

```

```

    user.must_change_password = True
```
Secure:
```
if user.credential_breached:
    user.must_change_password = True   # no time-based expiry
```

**Security Rule:** Passwords submitted at registration or password change must be checked against a set of known breached passwords.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** No breach check is run, so users adopt passwords already exposed in public data breaches and immediately vulnerable to credential stuffing.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The proposed password is checked against a breached-password dataset, for example via a k-anonymity range API or a local breach corpus, and rejected on a match.

**Security Rule:** Access to the application must require multi-factor authentication or a combination of single-factor mechanisms; for L3 one factor must be a phishing-resistant hardware authenticator that confirms user intent.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Authentication relies on a single factor — a password alone — so a phished or stuffed credential is enough to fully take over an account.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Login requires a second factor in addition to the password, such as a TOTP or a FIDO hardware authenticator, before a session is issued.

Vulnerable:

if (_hasher.Verify(user.Hash, password))
    return _sessions.Create(user);
```

```
if (_hasher.Verify(user.Hash, password)
    && _totp.Verify(user.TotpSecret, otpCode))
    return _sessions.Create(user);
```

Vulnerable:

Secure:

    issue_session(user)
```

```

    return sessionService.create(user);
```

```

```
Secure:
```

    verifyTotp(user.totpSecret, otpCode))

```

    return create_session(user)

   verify_totp(user.totp_secret, otp_code):
    return create_session(user)
```

**Security Rule:** When multiple authentication pathways exist there must be no undocumented paths, and security controls and authentication strength must be enforced consistently across all of them.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A forgotten or undocumented pathway, such as a legacy mobile token endpoint, skips MFA or rate limiting, giving an attacker a weaker route in.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** All authentication routes are inventoried and funnelled through a single policy layer so MFA, throttling, and lockout apply identically everywhere.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
[HttpPost("legacy/login")]      // not behind the MFA middleware
public IActionResult Legacy(Creds c) => Ok(PasswordOnly(c));
```
Secure:
```
[HttpPost("legacy/login")]
public IActionResult Legacy(Creds c) => Ok(_authPolicy.Run(c));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
web_login(user, pw, otp)         # enforces MFA
mobile_v1_login(user, pw)        # undocumented, skips MFA
```
Secure:
```
for path in all_login_paths(): authenticate(path, creds)  # one policy
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
@PostMapping("/legacy/login")   // bypasses the MFA filter
Token legacy(@RequestBody Creds c) { return passwordOnly(c); }
```
Secure:
```
@PostMapping("/legacy/login")
Token legacy(@RequestBody Creds c) { return authPolicy.run(c); }
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
@app.post("/api/v1/token")      # legacy, no MFA, undocumented
def legacy_token(data):
    return issue(authenticate_password_only(data))
```
Secure:
```
@app.post("/api/v1/token")
def token(data):
    return issue(authentication_policy.run(data))   # MFA enforced
```

When multiple authentication pathways exist there must be no undocumented paths, and security controls and authentication strength must be enforced consistently across all of them.
A forgotten or undocumented pathway, such as a legacy mobile token endpoint, skips MFA or rate limiting, giving an attacker a weaker route in.
All authentication routes are inventoried and funnelled through a single policy layer so MFA, throttling, and lockout apply identically everywhere.

Users must be notified of suspicious authentication attempts, whether successful or not, such as logins from an unusual location or client or a success following several failures.
The application never alerts users to anomalous logins, so an account takeover from a new device or country goes completely unnoticed.
Authentication events are evaluated for risk signals, and the user is sent an out-of-band notification when a login looks suspicious.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A login code is emailed and treated as an authentication factor, so whoever controls the often weakly protected mailbox can authenticate.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Authentication factors are limited to passwords plus a real second factor such as TOTP or a hardware authenticator; email is used only for non-authentication notifications.

**Security Rule:** System-generated initial passwords and activation codes must be securely randomly generated, comply with the password policy, expire after a short period or on first use, and never become the long-term password.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Initial passwords are predictable or never expire, so an unused or stale activation secret can be guessed or reused to take over an account.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Initial secrets are generated with a CSPRNG, carry a short expiry, are single-use, and force the user to set a new password before normal access.

```

```

```

store(code, Instant.now().plus(30, MINUTES), true);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

```

           single_use=True)

```

**Security Rule:** Renewal instructions for authentication mechanisms that expire must be sent with enough lead time to complete renewal before expiry, using automated reminders where needed.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Notice of an expiring authentication mechanism arrives only after it has lapsed, locking users out and pushing them toward risky recovery paths.

```

**Security Rule:** Lookup secrets, out-of-band codes, and time-based one-time passwords must be usable only once, so a captured or observed code cannot be replayed.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An OTP or recovery code remains valid after a successful use within its time window, letting an attacker who intercepts it replay it.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each code is marked consumed atomically on first successful verification and rejected on every subsequent attempt within its lifetime.

    && _usedCodes.TryMarkUsed(user.Id, code))
    Authenticate(user);
```

```

```
if code == expected and not consumed(code):

```

```

```
Secure:
```
if (totp.verify(user.getSecret(), code)
    && usedCodes.markUsedIfNew(user.getId(), code))
    authenticate(user);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]

```
if verify_totp(user.totp_secret, code):
    authenticate(user)
```
Secure:
```
if verify_totp(user.totp_secret, code) and \
   redis.set(f"otp:{user.id}:{code}", 1, nx=True, ex=90):
    authenticate(user)
```

Each code is marked consumed atomically on first successful verification and rejected on every subsequent attempt within its lifetime.

Lookup secrets stored in the backend with less than 112 bits of entropy must be hashed with an approved password-hashing algorithm using a 32-bit random salt; a standard hash is acceptable only at 112 bits or more.
Low-entropy lookup or recovery secrets are stored in plaintext or with a fast unsalted hash, so a database leak exposes them to immediate offline cracking.
Low-entropy secrets are stored using an approved slow password-hashing algorithm with a per-secret random salt; only high-entropy secrets may use a plain cryptographic hash.

**Security Rule:** Lookup secrets, out-of-band codes, and TOTP seeds must be generated with a cryptographically secure pseudorandom number generator so values are not predictable.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Codes or seeds are produced by a non-cryptographic PRNG or seeded by time, letting an attacker predict or reproduce them.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** All authentication secrets are drawn from a CSPRNG so every value is unpredictable and independent of any observable state.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var code = new Random().Next(0, 1000000);
```
Secure:
```
var code = RandomNumberGenerator.GetInt32(0, 1000000);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
code = random(100000, 999999)        # non-crypto PRNG
```
Secure:
```
code = csprng_int(100000, 999999)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
int code = new Random().nextInt(1000000);
```
Secure:
```
int code = SecureRandom.getInstanceStrong().nextInt(1000000);
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const code = Math.floor(Math.random() * 1000000);
```
Secure:
```
const code = crypto.randomInt(0, 1000000);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
import random
code = random.randint(100000, 999999)
```
Secure:
```
import secrets
code = secrets.randbelow(900000) + 100000
```
embed_text:
Lookup secrets, out-of-band codes, and TOTP seeds must be generated with a cryptographically secure pseudorandom number generator so values are not predictable.
Codes or seeds are produced by a non-cryptographic PRNG or seeded by time, letting an attacker predict or reproduce them.
All authentication secrets are drawn from a CSPRNG so every value is unpredictable and independent of any observable state.

**Security Rule:** Lookup secrets and out-of-band authentication codes must carry at least 20 bits of entropy, typically 4 random alphanumeric characters or 6 random digits.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Out-of-band codes are too short, for example a 3-digit code, leaving so few possibilities that brute force succeeds quickly.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Codes are generated with at least 20 bits of entropy, such as 6 random digits, so the search space is large enough to resist guessing.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var code = RandomNumberGenerator.GetInt32(1000).ToString("D3");
```
Secure:
```
var code = RandomNumberGenerator.GetInt32(1000000).ToString("D6");
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
code = csprng_digits(3)            # ~10 bits of entropy
```
Secure:
```
code = csprng_digits(6)            # ~20 bits of entropy
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
String code = String.format("%03d", rnd.nextInt(1000));
```
Secure:
```
String code = String.format("%06d", rnd.nextInt(1000000));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
code = f"{secrets.randbelow(1000):03d}"
```
Secure:
```
code = f"{secrets.randbelow(1000000):06d}"
```
embed_text:
Lookup secrets and out-of-band authentication codes must carry at least 20 bits of entropy, typically 4 random alphanumeric characters or 6 random digits.
Out-of-band codes are too short, for example a 3-digit code, leaving so few possibilities that brute force succeeds quickly.
Codes are generated with at least 20 bits of entropy, such as 6 random digits, so the search space is large enough to resist guessing.

**Security Rule:** Out-of-band requests, codes, and tokens must have a defined lifetime — at most 10 minutes for out-of-band requests and at most 30 seconds for TOTP codes.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An out-of-band code or token has no expiry, so an attacker can use a code captured hours or days earlier.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each code is stamped with a short expiry — 10 minutes or less for out-of-band, 30 seconds for TOTP — and rejected once that window passes.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
_otp.Save(new Otp(user.Id, code));
```
Secure:
```
_otp.Save(new Otp(user.Id, code, DateTime.UtcNow.AddMinutes(10)));
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
store(code)                                # no expiry
```
Secure:
```
store(code, expires_at=now() + minutes(10))
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
otpRepo.save(new Otp(user.getId(), code));
```
Secure:
```
otpRepo.save(new Otp(user.getId(), code,
                     Instant.now().plus(10, MINUTES)));
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
otp_store[user.id] = code
```
Secure:
```
otp_store[user.id] = (code, now() + timedelta(minutes=10))
# verification rejects when now() > expiry
```
embed_text:
Out-of-band requests, codes, and tokens must have a defined lifetime — at most 10 minutes for out-of-band requests and at most 30 seconds for TOTP codes.
An out-of-band code or token has no expiry, so an attacker can use a code captured hours or days earlier.
Each code is stamped with a short expiry — 10 minutes or less for out-of-band, 30 seconds for TOTP — and rejected once that window passes.

**Security Rule:** Any authentication factor, including physical devices, must be revocable in the event of theft or other loss.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** There is no way to revoke a registered authenticator, so a stolen hardware key or compromised device keeps working indefinitely.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each registered factor can be individually revoked, immediately invalidating it for future authentication.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
// no RevokeFactor action exists
```
Secure:
```
void RevokeFactor(User u, Guid factorId) {
    var f = _factors.Find(factorId);
    f.RevokedAt = DateTime.UtcNow;
}
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
# authenticators table has no revoked flag and no revoke path
```
Secure:
```
def revoke_factor(user, factor_id): mark_revoked(user, factor_id)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
// AuthFactor entity has no revoked field
```
Secure:
```
void revokeFactor(User u, String factorId) {
    authFactorRepo.findById(factorId)
        .ifPresent(f -> f.setRevokedAt(Instant.now()));
}
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
# no endpoint or column to disable a registered authenticator
```
Secure:
```
def revoke_factor(user, factor_id):
    f = AuthFactor.get(user.id, factor_id)
    f.revoked_at = now()
```
embed_text:
Any authentication factor, including physical devices, must be revocable in the event of theft or other loss.
There is no way to revoke a registered authenticator, so a stolen hardware key or compromised device keeps working indefinitely.
Each registered factor can be individually revoked, immediately invalidating it for future authentication.

**Security Rule:** Biometric authentication may be used only as a secondary factor, combined with either something you have or something you know.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A biometric such as a fingerprint or face scan is accepted as the sole factor, even though biometrics cannot be revoked and can be spoofed or compromised.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Biometric verification is paired with another factor, such as a password or a possession factor, so it never stands alone.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (_biometrics.Matches(scan, user.Template)) Authenticate(user);
```
Secure:
```
if (_biometrics.Matches(scan, user.Template)
    && _hasher.Verify(user.Hash, password))
    Authenticate(user);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if biometric_ok(scan): authenticate(user)
```
Secure:
```
if biometric_ok(scan) and verify(user.hash, password):
    authenticate(user)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (biometrics.matches(scan, user.getTemplate()))
    authenticate(user);
```
Secure:
```
if (biometrics.matches(scan, user.getTemplate())
    && encoder.matches(password, user.getHash()))
    authenticate(user);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if match_fingerprint(scan, user.template):
    authenticate(user)
```
Secure:
```
if match_fingerprint(scan, user.template) and \
   verify_password(user.hash, password):
    authenticate(user)
```
embed_text:
Biometric authentication may be used only as a secondary factor, combined with either something you have or something you know.
A biometric such as a fingerprint or face scan is accepted as the sole factor, even though biometrics cannot be revoked and can be spoofed or compromised.
Biometric verification is paired with another factor, such as a password or a possession factor, so it never stands alone.

**Security Rule:** TOTP codes must be validated against a time source from a trusted service, never against time supplied by an untrusted or client-provided source.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The server validates a TOTP using a timestamp sent by the client, so an attacker can pick a time window and replay a known code.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** TOTP verification uses the server's own trusted, synchronized clock and ignores any client-supplied time value.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
_totp.Verify(secret, code, long.Parse(Request.Query["ts"]));
```
Secure:
```
_totp.Verify(secret, code, DateTimeOffset.UtcNow.ToUnixTimeSeconds());
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
verify_totp(secret, code, at=request.client_time)
```
Secure:
```
verify_totp(secret, code, at=trusted_server_time())
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
totp.verify(secret, code, Long.parseLong(request.getParameter("ts")));
```
Secure:
```
totp.verify(secret, code, Instant.now().getEpochSecond());
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
totp.verify(code, for_time=int(request.args["ts"]))
```
Secure:
```
totp.verify(code, for_time=time.time())   # server clock only
```
embed_text:
TOTP codes must be validated against a time source from a trusted service, never against time supplied by an untrusted or client-provided source.
The server validates a TOTP using a timestamp sent by the client, so an attacker can pick a time window and replay a known code.
TOTP verification uses the server's own trusted, synchronized clock and ignores any client-supplied time value.

**Security Rule:** PSTN-delivered OTPs via phone or SMS may be offered only when the phone number was previously validated, stronger alternatives such as TOTP are also offered, and users are informed of the risks; for L3 phone and SMS must not be available.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** SMS is the only or default second factor, and is offered to unverified numbers, exposing users to SIM-swap and SS7 interception attacks.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** SMS OTP is offered only to validated numbers, alongside a stronger TOTP or hardware option, with a clear risk notice, and is disabled entirely for L3.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
IEnumerable<string> MfaOptions(User u) => new[] { "sms" };
```
Secure:
```
IEnumerable<string> MfaOptions(User u) {
    var opts = new List<string> { "totp", "webauthn" };
    if (u.PhoneVerified) opts.Add("sms");
    return opts;
}
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
offer_mfa_options(["sms"])              # SMS only, number unverified
```
Secure:
```
opts = ["totp", "hardware_key"]
if phone_verified(user): opts.append("sms")   # with risk notice
offer_mfa_options(opts)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
List<String> mfaOptions(User u) { return List.of("sms"); }
```
Secure:
```
List<String> mfaOptions(User u) {
    var opts = new ArrayList<>(List.of("totp", "webauthn"));
    if (u.isPhoneVerified()) opts.add("sms");
    return opts;
}
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
def mfa_options(user):
    return ["sms"]
```
Secure:
```
def mfa_options(user):
    opts = ["totp", "webauthn"]
    if user.phone_verified:
        opts.append("sms")   # shown with a SIM-swap risk warning
    return opts
```
embed_text:
PSTN-delivered OTPs via phone or SMS may be offered only when the phone number was previously validated, stronger alternatives such as TOTP are also offered, and users are informed of the risks; for L3 phone and SMS must not be available.
SMS is the only or default second factor, and is offered to unverified numbers, exposing users to SIM-swap and SS7 interception attacks.
SMS OTP is offered only to validated numbers, alongside a stronger TOTP or hardware option, with a clear risk notice, and is disabled entirely for L3.

**Security Rule:** Out-of-band requests, codes, and tokens must be bound to the specific authentication request that generated them and must not be valid for any earlier or later request.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** An out-of-band code is stored only against the user, so a code from one login attempt can be replayed to complete a different session.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each code is tied to a unique authentication request identifier and verified only against the session that created it.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
_cache.Set($"otp:{user.Id}", code);
```
Secure:
```
_cache.Set($"otp:{authRequestId}", code);
// bound to this authentication request only
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
otp_store[user.id] = code
if submitted == otp_store[user.id]: authenticate(user)
```
Secure:
```
otp_store[request_id] = code
if request_id == session.request_id and submitted == otp_store[request_id]:
    authenticate(user)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
cache.put("otp:" + user.getId(), code);
```
Secure:
```
cache.put("otp:" + authRequestId, code);
// verified against the session's own authRequestId
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
cache.set(f"otp:{user.id}", code)
```
Secure:
```
cache.set(f"otp:{auth_request_id}", code)
# verify only when auth_request_id matches the current session
```
embed_text:
Out-of-band requests, codes, and tokens must be bound to the specific authentication request that generated them and must not be valid for any earlier or later request.
An out-of-band code is stored only against the user, so a code from one login attempt can be replayed to complete a different session.
Each code is tied to a unique authentication request identifier and verified only against the session that created it.

A code-based out-of-band authentication mechanism must be protected against brute force with rate limiting, and ideally use a code with at least 64 bits of entropy.
Unlimited verification attempts are allowed against a short numeric code, so an attacker can iterate the entire code space in seconds.
Verification attempts are rate limited per code and per account, and the code is invalidated after a small number of failures.

**Security Rule:** Where push notifications are used for multi-factor authentication, rate limiting must prevent push-bombing attacks, and number matching may also be used to mitigate the risk.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Each login attempt fires an approval push with no limit, so an attacker floods the victim with prompts until one is approved out of fatigue.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Approval pushes are rate limited per account, and number matching requires the user to enter a code shown on the login screen, defeating blind approvals.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
void OnLogin(User u) => _push.Send(u, "Approve login?");
```
Secure:
```
void OnLogin(User u) {
    if (RecentPushes(u) >= 3) throw new TooManyRequestsException();
    _push.Send(u, $"Enter {challenge} to approve");
}
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
on_login_attempt(user): send_push(user)         # one per attempt
```
Secure:
```
if pushes_sent(user, last_minutes(10)) < 3:
    send_push_with_number_match(user, challenge)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
void onLogin(User u) { pushService.send(u, "Approve login?"); }
```
Secure:
```
void onLogin(User u) {
    if (recentPushes(u) >= 3) throw new TooManyRequestsException();
    pushService.send(u, "Enter " + challenge + " to approve");
}
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
def on_login(user):
    push_service.send(user, "Approve login?")
```
Secure:
```
def on_login(user):
    if recent_pushes(user) >= 3:
        raise TooManyRequests()
    push_service.send(user, f"Enter {challenge} to approve")
```
embed_text:
Where push notifications are used for multi-factor authentication, rate limiting must prevent push-bombing attacks, and number matching may also be used to mitigate the risk.
Each login attempt fires an approval push with no limit, so an attacker floods the victim with prompts until one is approved out of fatigue.
Approval pushes are rate limited per account, and number matching requires the user to enter a code shown on the login screen, defeating blind approvals.

**Security Rule:** Certificates used to verify cryptographic authentication assertions must be stored in a way that protects them from modification.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** Trusted verification certificates are kept in a world-writable file or mutable database row, so an attacker can swap in their own certificate and forge accepted assertions.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Verification certificates are held in an integrity-protected store such as a read-only trust store, a hardware security module, or a managed key vault with strict write controls.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var cert = new X509Certificate2(@"C:\temp\trusted.pem");
```
Secure:
```
using var store = new X509Store(StoreName.Root,
    StoreLocation.LocalMachine, OpenFlags.ReadOnly);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
cert = read_file("/tmp/trusted.pem")        # writable by anyone
```
Secure:
```
cert = trust_store.load("auth-verify")      # integrity-protected store
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
X509Certificate c = loadPem(new File("/tmp/trusted.pem"));
```
Secure:
```
KeyStore ks = KeyStore.getInstance("PKCS11");   // HSM-backed
X509Certificate c = (X509Certificate) ks.getCertificate("auth-verify");
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
with open("/var/data/trusted.pem") as f:    # 0666 permissions
    cert = load_pem_x509_certificate(f.read().encode())
```
Secure:
```
cert = key_vault.get_certificate("auth-verify")   # managed, immutable
```
embed_text:
Certificates used to verify cryptographic authentication assertions must be stored in a way that protects them from modification.
Trusted verification certificates are kept in a world-writable file or mutable database row, so an attacker can swap in their own certificate and forge accepted assertions.
Verification certificates are held in an integrity-protected store such as a read-only trust store, a hardware security module, or a managed key vault with strict write controls.

**Security Rule:** The challenge nonce used in cryptographic authentication must be at least 64 bits long and statistically unique, or unique over the lifetime of the cryptographic device.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The authentication challenge is too short or reused, so a captured signed response can be replayed to authenticate again.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Each challenge is a fresh nonce of at least 64 bits drawn from a CSPRNG, making replay of a previously signed response infeasible.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var challenge = Encoding.UTF8.GetBytes(counter++.ToString());
```
Secure:
```
var challenge = RandomNumberGenerator.GetBytes(16);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
challenge = counter                       # short and predictable
```
Secure:
```
challenge = csprng_bytes(16)              # 128-bit unique nonce
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
byte[] challenge = Long.toString(counter++).getBytes();
```
Secure:
```
byte[] challenge = new byte[16];
SecureRandom.getInstanceStrong().nextBytes(challenge);
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
challenge = str(int(time.time()))
```
Secure:
```
import secrets
challenge = secrets.token_bytes(16)       # 128 bits from a CSPRNG
```
embed_text:
The challenge nonce used in cryptographic authentication must be at least 64 bits long and statistically unique, or unique over the lifetime of the cryptographic device.
The authentication challenge is too short or reused, so a captured signed response can be replayed to authenticate again.
Each challenge is a fresh nonce of at least 64 bits drawn from a CSPRNG, making replay of a previously signed response infeasible.

**Security Rule:** When multiple identity providers are supported, a user's identity must not be spoofable through another provider; users should be keyed on the IdP identifier combined with the user's ID at that IdP.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The application matches federated users by email or bare user ID alone, so an attacker registering the same identifier at a different IdP takes over the account.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Accounts are keyed on the pair of IdP identifier and the subject claim from that IdP, so identifiers from different providers never collide.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var u = _users.SingleOrDefault(x => x.Email == claims.Email);
```
Secure:
```
var u = _users.SingleOrDefault(x =>
    x.Idp == providerId && x.IdpSubject == claims.Subject);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
user = find_user(email=claims.email)
```
Secure:
```
user = find_user(idp_id=provider.id, subject=claims.sub)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
User u = userRepo.findByEmail(claims.getEmail());
```
Secure:
```
User u = userRepo.findByIdpAndSubject(providerId, claims.getSubject());
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const user = await User.findOne({ email: claims.email });
```
Secure:
```
const user = await User.findOne({
  idp: providerId, idpSubject: claims.sub });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
user = User.query.filter_by(email=id_token["email"]).first()
```
Secure:
```
user = User.query.filter_by(
    idp=provider_id, idp_subject=id_token["sub"]).first()
```
embed_text:
When multiple identity providers are supported, a user's identity must not be spoofable through another provider; users should be keyed on the IdP identifier combined with the user's ID at that IdP.
The application matches federated users by email or bare user ID alone, so an attacker registering the same identifier at a different IdP takes over the account.
Accounts are keyed on the pair of IdP identifier and the subject claim from that IdP, so identifiers from different providers never collide.

**Security Rule:** The presence and integrity of digital signatures on authentication assertions such as JWTs or SAML assertions must always be validated, and any unsigned or invalidly signed assertion must be rejected.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** The application decodes a JWT or SAML assertion without verifying its signature, or accepts the alg none value, so an attacker can forge arbitrary claims.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** Every assertion is verified against the expected signing key and algorithm before any claim is trusted, and unsigned assertions are rejected.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
var jwt = new JwtSecurityToken(token);   // no signature check
```
Secure:
```
var p = new TokenValidationParameters {
    ValidateIssuerSigningKey = true, IssuerSigningKey = idpKey };
handler.ValidateToken(token, p, out _);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
claims = jwt_decode(token, verify=false)
```
Secure:
```
claims = jwt_decode(token, key=idp_public_key, algorithms=["RS256"])
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
Claims c = Jwts.parser().parseClaimsJwt(unsignedToken).getBody();
```
Secure:
```
Claims c = Jwts.parser().verifyWith(idpPublicKey).build()
               .parseSignedClaims(token).getPayload();
```

[[JAVASCRIPT PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
const claims = jwt.decode(token);
```
Secure:
```
const claims = jwt.verify(token, idpPublicKey, { algorithms: ["RS256"] });
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
claims = jwt.decode(token, options={"verify_signature": False})
```
Secure:
```
claims = jwt.decode(token, idp_public_key, algorithms=["RS256"])
```
embed_text:
The presence and integrity of digital signatures on authentication assertions such as JWTs or SAML assertions must always be validated, and any unsigned or invalidly signed assertion must be rejected.
The application decodes a JWT or SAML assertion without verifying its signature, or accepts the alg none value, so an attacker can forge arbitrary claims.
Every assertion is verified against the expected signing key and algorithm before any claim is trusted, and unsigned assertions are rejected.

**Security Rule:** SAML assertions must be processed uniquely and used only once within their validity period, to prevent replay attacks.

**Vulnerability Pattern (Description):** A SAML assertion is accepted on every presentation within its validity window, so an attacker who captures one can replay it to log in.

**Secure Pattern (Description):** The assertion ID is recorded on first use and any later presentation of the same ID within the validity window is rejected as a replay.

[[CSHARP PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (assertion.IsWithinValidity()) Login(assertion.Subject);
```
Secure:
```
if (assertion.IsWithinValidity()
    && _replayCache.TryRecord(assertion.Id))
    Login(assertion.Subject);
```

[[GENERIC PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if valid_window(assertion): authenticate(assertion.subject)
```
Secure:
```
if valid_window(assertion) and not seen(assertion.id):
    record_seen(assertion.id); authenticate(assertion.subject)
```

[[JAVA PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```
if (assertion.isWithinValidity()) login(assertion.getSubject());
```
Secure:
```
if (assertion.isWithinValidity()
    && replayCache.recordIfNew(assertion.getId()))
    login(assertion.getSubject());
```

[[PYTHON PATTERNS]]
Vulnerable:
```

    log_in(assertion.subject)
```

   cache.set(f"saml:{assertion.id}", 1, nx=True, ex=600):

```
